meta.name: Build Your Own SQLite
meta.description: Embedded SQL database with tokenizer, parser, bytecode compiler (VDBE), page-based B-tree/B+tree storage, buffer pool, query planner, and ACID transactions via rollback journal and WAL.
meta.essence: SQL tokenization and recursive-descent parsing producing ASTs, compiled into bytecode instructions executed by a virtual machine (VDBE), operating over a page-based storage engine with B-trees for clustered table storage and B+trees for secondary indexes, managed through a buffer pool with LRU eviction, and ACID guarantees via either rollback journal or write-ahead logging.
meta.why_important: Building a database from scratch teaches the fundamental data structures and algorithms underlying all modern databases. These skills—disk I/O management, B-tree indexing, bytecode execution, query optimization, and crash recovery— are directly applicable to backend engineering, distributed systems, and performance optimization roles.
learning_outcomes: Build a lexer and recursive-descent parser converting SQL text into an AST
Compile ASTs into bytecode instructions for a virtual machine (VDBE)
Implement a buffer pool manager with LRU eviction and dirty page tracking
Design page-based B-tree storage for tables (clustered) and B+tree for indexes
Implement row storage with variable-length record encoding
Build a cost-based query planner with statistics-driven cardinality estimation
Implement ACID transactions with rollback journal for crash recovery
Design WAL mode for improved concurrent read/write performance

milestones.name: SQL Tokenizer
milestones.description: Build a lexer that converts SQL text into a stream of typed tokens.
acceptance_criteria: Tokenizer recognizes SQL keywords (SELECT, INSERT, CREATE, WHERE, JOIN, etc.) case-insensitively
String literals enclosed in single quotes are parsed including escaped quotes ('it''s' → it's)
Numeric literals including integers and floating-point values (42, 3.14, -7) are recognized as distinct token types
Operators (=, <, >, <=, >=, !=, <>) and punctuation (comma, parentheses, semicolon) are tokenized as distinct tokens
Identifiers (table names, column names) support quoted identifiers with double quotes ("column name")
Tokenizer reports error position (line and column) for unrecognized characters
Token stream correctly tokenizes at least 20 diverse SQL statements in a test suite
Tokenizer recognizes keywords like SELECT and INSERT case-insensitively
String literals correctly handle escaped single quotes ('') and preserve internal content
Numeric literals distinguish between integers (42) and floats (3.14)
Double-quoted identifiers ('"Table Name"') are correctly tokenized as identifiers
The tokenizer returns a list or stream of objects containing type, value, line, and column
A test suite of 20+ SQL statements produces the expected token sequence
pitfalls: Not handling escaped quotes in string literals ('it''s') causes premature string termination
Keywords must be case-insensitive but identifiers may be case-sensitive depending on quoting—handle both
Unicode identifiers require careful handling; start simple with ASCII and document limitations
Negative numbers may be ambiguous with subtraction operator; handle at parser level, not tokenizer
concepts: Lexical analysis converts character stream to token stream
Finite state machine drives character-by-character token recognition
Token types classify each lexeme (keyword, identifier, literal, operator)
Error reporting with source location enables useful diagnostics
deliverables: Lexer producing token stream from SQL input string
Keyword recognition for all supported SQL words
String and numeric literal parsing with escape handling
Operator and punctuation tokenization
Error reporting with line and column position
Test suite with at least 20 SQL statements

milestones.name: Bytecode Compiler (VDBE)
milestones.description: Compile parsed AST into bytecode instructions executed by a virtual machine. This is the execution engine of the database.
acceptance_criteria: Compiler translates SELECT AST into a bytecode program with opcodes for OpenTable, Rewind, Column, ResultRow, Next, Halt
Compiler translates INSERT AST into bytecode with opcodes for OpenTable, MakeRecord, Insert, Halt
Virtual machine executes bytecode programs step-by-step, processing one opcode per cycle
VM maintains a register file (array of typed values) for intermediate computation
EXPLAIN command outputs the bytecode program for a given SQL statement in human-readable format
WHERE clause compiles to conditional jump opcodes that skip non-matching rows
Bytecode execution of 'SELECT * FROM t' on a 10,000-row table completes in under 100ms
Compiler translates SELECT AST into opcodes including OpenTable, Rewind, Column, ResultRow, Next, and Halt
Compiler translates INSERT AST into opcodes including OpenTable, MakeRecord, and Insert
VM executes bytecode in a fetch-decode-execute loop, processing one opcode per cycle
VM manages a register file of typed values for intermediate calculations

concepts: Bytecode compilation translates high-level AST into low-level instruction sequence
Virtual machine executes bytecode using a fetch-decode-execute loop

deliverables: Bytecode instruction set with opcodes for table operations, comparisons, jumps, and output

milestones.name: Buffer Pool Manager
milestones.description: Implement a page cache that sits between the B-tree layer and disk, managing fixed-size pages with LRU eviction and dirty page tracking.
acceptance_criteria: Buffer pool manages a configurable number of in-memory page frames (default 1000 pages)

FetchPage loads a page from disk into a free frame, or returns the cached frame if already resident

Dirty page tracking marks pages modified in memory; eviction writes dirty pages to disk before replacement
Pin/Unpin mechanism prevents eviction of pages currently in use by B-tree operations
FlushAll writes all dirty pages to disk (used before checkpoint or shutdown)
Buffer pool hit rate is measurable and logged for performance tuning
Buffer pool initializes with a fixed number of 4096-byte frames
FetchPage returns the correct page from memory if already loaded (hit)
FetchPage loads page from disk if not in memory (miss)
LRU algorithm correctly identifies the least recently used page for eviction
Pinned pages (count > 0) are never selected for eviction
Dirty pages are written back to disk only when evicted or on FlushAll

pitfalls: Evicting a pinned page causes data corruption or use-after-free—enforce pin counting
Not flushing dirty pages before eviction loses committed data
Page ID collisions if page numbering is not globally unique across the database file

concepts: Buffer pool caches disk pages in memory for fast access
LRU (Least Recently Used) eviction approximates optimal page replacement

Dirty page tracking enables write-back caching
deliverables: Buffer pool with configurable frame count and page size

blueprint.title: Build Your Own SQLite
blueprint.overview:
This project constructs a fully functional embedded SQL database engine from first principles — spanning the entire pipeline from raw SQL text to durable, crash-recoverable on-disk storage. You will build a tokenizer and recursive-descent parser that produce an AST, compile that AST into bytecode instructions executed by a register-based virtual machine (the VDBE), layer a buffer pool manager over page-based disk I/O, implement B-tree and B+tree storage for tables and indexes, build a cost-based query planner, and finally implement ACID transactions via both rollback journal and write-ahead logging.

The architecture mirrors real SQLite's layered design: a frontend (tokenizer → parser → compiler), a virtual machine execution engine, and a backend (buffer pool → B-tree → pager → OS interface). Each layer communicates through narrow, well-defined interfaces — the VM never touches disk directly, and the B-tree never parses SQL. This separation of concerns is what makes SQLite's ~150K lines of C manageable, and what makes this project teachable.

By the end, you will have internalized the fundamental tension of all storage systems — speed vs. durability — and understood why every database in existence makes the same handful of architectural choices, just with different tradeoffs.
blueprint.design_philosophy: This project teaches by building a complete vertical slice of database engineering. Rather than studying components in isolation, each milestone extends a working system — you can always run SQL against your engine, even if it's incomplete. The misconception-reveal pairs are designed to shatter the abstraction barriers that prevent most developers from reasoning about database behavior: why queries are slow, why crashes don't lose data, why indexes sometimes make things worse. Every milestone unlocks understanding that transfers to PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, and distributed databases.

implementation.rationale: SQLite itself is written in C, and the project's core challenges — page-level binary formats, pointer arithmetic for slotted pages, manual buffer pool management, fsync semantics, and byte-level record encoding — are most naturally expressed in C. The language forces you to confront memory layout, endianness, and OS-level I/O directly, which is the entire point of building a storage engine. No garbage collector hides the buffer pool's eviction decisions; no runtime hides the cost of a page fault.
implementation.style_guide: snake_case for functions and variables. UPPER_CASE for constants and opcodes. Prefix public APIs with module name (e.g., pager_fetch_page, btree_insert, vm_execute). Structs use typedef with PascalCase (e.g., typedef struct { ... } BufferPool;). Header files use include guards. All functions that can fail return a status code (SQLITE_OK, SQLITE_ERROR, etc.).
implementation.build_system: Makefile with separate compilation units per module (tokenizer.c, parser.c, vm.c, pager.c, btree.c, wal.c). Debug build with -g -fsanitize=address. Test target runs all test suites.
alternatives: Rust — excellent for safety guarantees on unsafe page manipulation, but adds borrow-checker friction for the self-referential B-tree structures
Go — good for the networking layer if you extend to client-server, but GC interference makes buffer pool behavior less transparent

assumed_known: C programming: pointers, structs, memory allocation (malloc/free), file I/O (open/read/write/lseek/close)
Basic SQL: SELECT, INSERT, CREATE TABLE, WHERE clauses
Binary data: bit shifting, endianness concepts, reading/writing binary files
Data structures: arrays, linked lists, hash tables, binary search
Tree data structures: binary trees, tree traversal (in-order, pre-order)
Basic compiler concepts: what a lexer and parser do at a high level
Command line: make, gcc, basic debugging

milestones.title: SQL Tokenizer
milestones.summary: Build a lexer that converts raw SQL text into a stream of typed tokens, handling keywords, identifiers, string/numeric literals, operators, and error reporting with source positions.
milestones.misconception: Developers assume tokenization is trivial string splitting — just split on whitespace and punctuation. They think regex or split() can handle SQL lexing.
milestones.reveal: SQL tokenization is a stateful process requiring a finite state machine. A single quote inside a string literal ('it''s') looks identical to a string terminator. The minus sign could be a negative number, a subtraction operator, or part of a comment (--). Context determines meaning, and only a state machine tracks context correctly.
cascade: Finite state machines — the same FSM concept powers network protocol parsers, regex engines, and hardware design (cross-domain: digital logic)
Error recovery in compilers — knowing the exact line/column of a bad token is how IDEs provide red squiggly underlines, connecting lexer design to developer tooling
Token stream as abstraction boundary — the parser never sees raw characters, enabling the tokenizer to be swapped (e.g., for a different SQL dialect) without changing the parser
Lookahead and buffering — peeking at the next character without consuming it is the same pattern used in network stream processing and file format parsing
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Tokenizer recognizes SQL keywords (SELECT, INSERT, CREATE, WHERE, JOIN, etc.) case-insensitively
String literals enclosed in single quotes are parsed including escaped quotes ('it''s' → it's)
Numeric literals including integers and floating-point values (42, 3.14, -7) are recognized as distinct token types
Operators (=, <, >, <=, >=, !=, <>) and punctuation (comma, parentheses, semicolon) are tokenized as distinct tokens
Identifiers (table names, column names) support quoted identifiers with double quotes ("column name")
Tokenizer reports error position (line and column) for unrecognized characters
Token stream correctly tokenizes at least 20 diverse SQL statements in a test suite

milestones.title: SQL Parser (AST)
milestones.summary: Build a recursive-descent parser that consumes the token stream and produces an Abstract Syntax Tree for SELECT, INSERT, and CREATE TABLE statements, with correct operator precedence and error reporting.
milestones.misconception: Developers think parsing is just matching patterns — if you see SELECT, grab everything until FROM, then grab until WHERE. They expect a flat, sequential approach will work.
milestones.reveal: SQL expressions are recursive. A WHERE clause can contain nested subexpressions, parenthesized groups, and operators with different binding strengths (NOT > comparison > AND > OR). A recursive-descent parser mirrors the grammar's recursive structure: each grammar rule becomes a function that calls other rule functions. Left recursion — the silent killer — causes infinite loops and requires restructuring via precedence climbing or Pratt parsing.
cascade: Grammar-driven design — every recursive-descent parser is a direct encoding of a BNF grammar, connecting database frontends to programming language compilers (cross-domain: PL theory)
Operator precedence as a general concept — the same precedence climbing technique applies to arithmetic, boolean logic, and even CSS specificity rules
AST as universal IR — the same AST pattern is used in JavaScript engines (V8), linters (ESLint), and code formatters (Prettier), making this a transferable architecture
Error recovery strategies — how the parser handles unexpected tokens determines UX quality; skip-and-resync is used in IDEs for real-time syntax highlighting
yaml_acceptance_criteria: SELECT parser produces AST with column list (including *), FROM clause, optional WHERE, ORDER BY, and LIMIT
INSERT parser produces AST with target table, optional column names, and VALUES clause
CREATE TABLE parser extracts column definitions with names, types (INTEGER, TEXT, REAL, BLOB), and constraints (PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
Expression parser correctly handles operator precedence: NOT > comparison (=,<,>) > AND > OR
Parenthesized expressions override default precedence
Parser produces clear error messages with token position for syntax errors
Test suite covers at least 15 valid and 10 invalid SQL statements

milestones.title: Bytecode Compiler (VDBE)
milestones.summary: Compile parsed AST into bytecode instructions executed by a register-based virtual machine. This is the execution engine — the VDBE — that bridges SQL semantics and storage operations.
milestones.misconception: Developers assume a database executes SQL by walking the AST directly — evaluate the WHERE node, then the SELECT node, like an interpreter. They think bytecode compilation is unnecessary complexity.
milestones.reveal: Tree-walking interpretation is O(tree-depth) overhead per row. For a 10,000-row scan, you re-navigate the AST tree 10,000 times. Bytecode flattens the tree into a linear instruction sequence — the VM just increments a program counter. More importantly, bytecode decouples the frontend (SQL dialects, parser changes) from the backend (storage engine). SQLite's VDBE has ~180 opcodes; every SQL feature compiles to combinations of these primitives. This is the same architecture as the JVM and CPython.
cascade: Virtual machines everywhere — the JVM, CPython, Lua, and WebAssembly all use the same fetch-decode-execute pattern you're building here (cross-domain: language runtimes)
Register allocation — deciding which VM register holds which intermediate value is the same problem CPU compilers solve, connecting databases to compiler backends
Cursor abstraction — opcodes like OpenTable/Rewind/Next create an iterator interface over B-trees, which is the same Iterator pattern from GoF design patterns
EXPLAIN as debuggability — making the bytecode human-readable is the same principle as disassemblers (objdump) and query plan visualizers in PostgreSQL
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Compiler translates SELECT AST into a bytecode program with opcodes for OpenTable, Rewind, Column, ResultRow, Next, Halt
Compiler translates INSERT AST into bytecode with opcodes for OpenTable, MakeRecord, Insert, Halt
Virtual machine executes bytecode programs step-by-step, processing one opcode per cycle
VM maintains a register file (array of typed values) for intermediate computation
EXPLAIN command outputs the bytecode program for a given SQL statement in human-readable format
WHERE clause compiles to conditional jump opcodes that skip non-matching rows
Bytecode execution of 'SELECT * FROM t' on a 10,000-row table completes in under 100ms

milestones.title: Buffer Pool Manager
milestones.summary: Implement a page cache between the B-tree layer and disk I/O, managing fixed-size pages in memory with LRU eviction, pin counting, and dirty page tracking.
milestones.misconception: Developers assume the OS page cache (via mmap or normal read/write) is sufficient — why build your own cache? They think the OS already handles caching optimally.
milestones.reveal: The OS page cache is a general-purpose LRU that knows nothing about your access patterns. A database's buffer pool knows which pages are pinned by active transactions, which pages will be needed next (via query plan lookahead), and critically — which pages are dirty and must be flushed in a specific order for crash safety. mmap gives you no control over eviction order, no dirty tracking, and no write ordering. LMDB uses mmap successfully but accepts significant tradeoffs. SQLite, PostgreSQL, and MySQL all manage their own buffer pools for these reasons.
cascade: Cache hierarchy — your buffer pool is one layer in a hierarchy (CPU L1/L2/L3 → RAM → buffer pool → OS page cache → disk), and the same eviction principles apply at every level (cross-domain: computer architecture)
Pin counting as reference counting — the same pattern used in shared_ptr (C++), Arc (Rust), and garbage collectors for preventing premature deallocation
Write-back vs write-through caching — the buffer pool uses write-back (defer writes); CPU caches use both strategies, and understanding this connects to coherence protocols in multi-core systems
Clock algorithm as LRU approximation — real implementations rarely use true LRU (too expensive); the Clock algorithm gives ~95% of LRU's hit rate at O(1) cost
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Buffer pool manages a configurable number of in-memory page frames (default 1000 pages)
Pages are fixed-size (4096 bytes by default, configurable)
FetchPage loads a page from disk into a free frame, or returns the cached frame if already resident
LRU eviction selects the least recently used unpinned page for replacement when no free frames exist
Dirty page tracking marks pages modified in memory; eviction writes dirty pages to disk before replacement
Pin/Unpin mechanism prevents eviction of pages currently in use by B-tree operations
FlushAll writes all dirty pages to disk (used before checkpoint or shutdown)
Buffer pool hit rate is measurable and logged for performance tuning

milestones.title: B-tree Page Format & Table Storage
milestones.summary: Implement the on-disk page structure for B-trees (tables) and B+trees (indexes), with slotted page layout, variable-length record encoding, node splitting, and a system catalog.
milestones.misconception: Developers think B-trees and B+trees are the same thing — just balanced search trees stored on disk. They conflate the textbook binary B-tree with the page-based multi-way B-tree used in databases.
milestones.reveal: The critical difference: in a B-tree (used for SQLite's table storage), every node — internal and leaf — contains full row data. In a B+tree (used for indexes), only leaf nodes contain data; internal nodes contain only separator keys and child pointers. This distinction determines everything: B+trees have higher fanout (more keys per internal page = shallower tree), support efficient range scans via linked leaf pages, but require a double lookup (index → rowid → table B-tree) for non-covering queries. SQLite's choice to use B-tree for tables and B+tree for indexes is a deliberate tradeoff between point lookup speed and range scan efficiency.
cascade: Fanout determines tree height — a B+tree with 4KB pages and 8-byte keys fits ~500 keys per internal node, meaning 500^3 = 125 million rows in just 3 levels. This is why databases feel fast (cross-domain: information theory / Shannon)

Slotted pages as a general storage pattern — the same bidirectional-growth layout (pointers from top, data from bottom) is used in PostgreSQL's heap pages, operating system memory allocators, and even some file systems
Variable-length encoding (varint) — the same technique used in Protocol Buffers, UTF-8, and LEB128 in DWARF debug info, connecting database internals to serialization formats everywhere

Node splitting as distributed consensus — splitting a B-tree node and promoting a key is structurally similar to a two-phase commit: the parent must acknowledge the new child before the operation is complete
System catalog as self-description — storing schema in the database itself (sqlite_master) is the same reflexive pattern as Python's __dict__ or Java's reflection API
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Page header contains page type (leaf/internal, table/index), cell count, free space pointer, and right-child pointer (internal only)
Table B-tree leaf pages store rows keyed by rowid with variable-length record encoding
Table B-tree internal pages store rowid separator keys and child page numbers
Index B+tree leaf pages store (indexed column value, rowid) pairs with data only in leaves
Index B+tree internal pages store only separator keys and child pointers (no row data)
CREATE TABLE creates a B-tree root page and records the schema in a system catalog (sqlite_master equivalent)
INSERT serializes a row and inserts into the correct B-tree leaf; node splitting creates a new page and promotes a separator key
Full table scan traverses all leaf pages in rowid order, returning all rows
Pages serialize to and deserialize from exactly 4096-byte buffers via the buffer pool

milestones.title: SELECT Execution & DML
milestones.summary: Execute SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE through the bytecode VM, with row deserialization, column projection, WHERE filtering using three-valued logic, and constraint enforcement.
milestones.misconception: Developers assume NULL is just 'empty' or 'zero' — a placeholder value that behaves like any other. They write WHERE col = NULL expecting it to work.
milestones.reveal: NULL means 'unknown,' and SQL uses three-valued logic: TRUE, FALSE, and NULL. The expression NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL (not TRUE), because you can't know if two unknown values are equal. This propagates: WHERE NULL AND TRUE is NULL (falsy), so the row is excluded. Every comparison, every boolean operator, every aggregate function must handle this third truth value. This is why IS NULL exists as a separate operator. Getting three-valued logic wrong corrupts query results silently — no crash, just wrong answers.
cascade: Three-valued logic in other domains — SQL's NULL semantics mirror the 'unknown' state in Kleene's three-valued logic from mathematical logic, and appear in hardware (tri-state buffers: high, low, high-impedance) (cross-domain: digital electronics)
Cursor-based iteration — the pattern of open/rewind/next/close for B-tree traversal is identical to database cursors in application code and iterator patterns in functional programming
Predicate pushdown — evaluating WHERE during scan (not after) is the simplest form of query optimization; the same principle drives filter pushdown in Spark and BigQuery
Delete-during-iteration problem — modifying a data structure while iterating it is a universal bug source, from ConcurrentModificationException in Java to iterator invalidation in C++ STL
yaml_acceptance_criteria: SELECT * FROM table returns all rows in rowid order via B-tree leaf scan
SELECT col1, col2 returns only specified columns (projection)
WHERE clause filters rows during scan, evaluating boolean expressions on deserialized column values
INSERT adds a row to the B-tree; subsequent SELECT returns the inserted data
UPDATE modifies columns in rows matching WHERE; subsequent SELECT reflects changes
DELETE removes rows matching WHERE; subsequent SELECT no longer returns them
NOT NULL constraint rejects INSERT or UPDATE setting a NOT NULL column to null
Operations on non-existent tables return an error with the table name

milestones.title: Secondary Indexes
milestones.summary: Implement secondary indexes using B+trees, integrate index lookups into query execution, and maintain index consistency across all DML operations.
milestones.misconception: Developers think an index is just a sorted copy of a column — a simple lookup table. They assume adding indexes always makes queries faster.
milestones.reveal: A secondary index is a separate B+tree whose keys are the indexed column values and whose leaf payloads are rowids pointing back to the table B-tree. Every query using an index performs a double lookup: first traverse the index B+tree to find the rowid, then traverse the table B-tree to fetch the full row. This double lookup means index scans can be SLOWER than full table scans when selectivity is poor (>20% of rows match). Furthermore, every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE must update ALL indexes on the table, turning one write into N+1 writes. Indexes are a read-write tradeoff, not a free speedup.
cascade: Covering indexes — if all columns needed by a query are in the index, the double lookup is eliminated. This is why PostgreSQL's INCLUDE clause and SQL Server's covering indexes exist, connecting to query planner cost models
Write amplification — each index doubles the write cost, which is the same amplification problem in LSM-trees (RocksDB/LevelDB) and SSDs (cross-domain: flash storage physics)
Index selection as an optimization problem — choosing which indexes to create is NP-hard in the general case; this connects to the database tuning advisor problem and operations research
Inverted indexes — search engines (Lucene, Elasticsearch) use the same key→pointer concept but with term→document_id lists instead of column_value→rowid (cross-domain: information retrieval)
yaml_acceptance_criteria: CREATE INDEX builds a B+tree index mapping (indexed column value → rowid) from existing table data
Index is automatically maintained on INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE (index entries added/removed/updated)
Index lookup retrieves rows matching an equality predicate without full table scan, verified by counting pages read
Range scan on index returns rows within a value range using B+tree leaf traversal
Query execution uses index scan when an indexed column appears in WHERE with equality or range predicate
UNIQUE index rejects INSERT or UPDATE that would create duplicate values

milestones.title: Query Planner & Statistics
milestones.summary: Implement a cost-based query planner that chooses between table scan and index scan based on statistics collected by ANALYZE, with join ordering for multi-table queries.
milestones.misconception: Developers think the query planner is a simple rule engine — 'if there's an index on the WHERE column, use it.' They assume optimal plans can be determined by syntax alone.
milestones.reveal: Optimal query plans depend on data distribution, not syntax. A query WHERE status = 'active' on a table where 95% of rows are active should use a full table scan (sequential I/O) rather than an index scan (95% × random I/O per row). The planner needs statistics — row counts, distinct value counts, histograms — to estimate selectivity and convert it to I/O cost. Without ANALYZE, the planner is guessing. With stale statistics, it's guessing confidently but wrong. Cardinality estimation errors compound exponentially through joins: a 10× error in one table becomes 100× after a two-table join, potentially choosing a plan that's 1000× slower.
cascade: Statistics as a first-class concern — every production database (PostgreSQL's pg_statistic, MySQL's InnoDB statistics) maintains histogram data, connecting database internals to probability and statistics (cross-domain: data science)

Dynamic programming for join ordering — the planner's search over join permutations uses the same DP technique as the Bellman-Ford shortest path algorithm and the knapsack problem (cross-domain: algorithms/OR)

Plan regression — when statistics change, a previously-fast query can become slow. This is why PostgreSQL has plan freezing and Oracle has SQL Plan Management, connecting to the broader problem of system stability

Adaptive query execution — modern systems (Spark AQE, SQL Server adaptive joins) re-plan mid-execution based on actual cardinalities, representing a convergence of compile-time and runtime optimization
yaml_acceptance_criteria: ANALYZE command collects statistics: row count per table, distinct value count per indexed column
Cost model estimates pages read for full table scan (total_pages) and index scan (estimated_rows / rows_per_page)
Planner selects index scan when estimated selectivity (matching_rows / total_rows) is below a threshold (e.g., 20%)
Planner falls back to table scan when no suitable index exists or selectivity is too low
EXPLAIN shows the chosen plan including scan type, index name (if used), and estimated row count
For multi-table queries (JOIN), planner estimates join cardinality and selects join order to minimize intermediate result size

milestones.title: Transactions (Rollback Journal)
milestones.summary: Implement ACID transactions using a rollback journal that records original page images before modification, with strict write ordering for crash recovery.
milestones.misconception: Developers think crash safety means 'just call fsync after every write.' They assume that writing data to a file means it's durable, and that crashes only happen between complete operations.
milestones.reveal: Crashes can happen mid-write. A 4KB page write can be torn — half-written — if power fails during the disk operation. fsync guarantees only that previously completed writes are durable, not that the current write is atomic. The rollback journal solves this by recording the ORIGINAL page contents before any modification. The write ordering protocol is inviolable: (1) write original pages to journal, (2) fsync journal, (3) write modified pages to database, (4) delete journal. If a crash occurs at any point, recovery is deterministic: if a journal exists, the transaction was incomplete, so restore original pages. This protocol transforms a non-atomic operation (page write) into an atomic transaction.
cascade: Write ordering as a universal durability pattern — the same before-image journaling is used in NTFS (log-structured), ext4 (journaling), and ZFS (copy-on-write), connecting database transactions to file system design (cross-domain: OS/file systems)
Torn page detection — the same problem exists in SSD firmware (FTL must handle interrupted program operations) and distributed systems (partial message delivery) (cross-domain: hardware reliability)

Undo vs redo logging — the rollback journal is an undo log (stores old values). WAL is a redo log (stores new values). Understanding both is understanding the full spectrum of crash recovery, which applies to every durable system from databases to message queues

Two-phase commit similarity — the journal acts as a prepare phase, the database write as a commit phase. The same two-phase structure appears in distributed transactions (2PC) and even in double-checked locking

fsync as a trust boundary — everything above fsync in the stack assumes durability; everything below (disk firmware, battery-backed cache) can violate that assumption. This connects to the broader concept of trust boundaries in security
yaml_acceptance_criteria: BEGIN starts a transaction; all subsequent writes are buffered until COMMIT or ROLLBACK
COMMIT makes all changes permanent by flushing dirty pages and removing the rollback journal
ROLLBACK undoes all changes by restoring original pages from the rollback journal
Rollback journal records original page contents BEFORE modification (for undo on crash)
Changes are not visible to other connections until COMMIT (basic read isolation)
Crash recovery on startup detects an existing rollback journal and automatically rolls back the incomplete transaction
Journal file is fsync'd before modified pages are written to the database file (write ordering guarantee)

milestones.title: WAL Mode
milestones.summary: Implement Write-Ahead Logging as an alternative to rollback journal, enabling concurrent readers during writes through append-only logging and snapshot isolation.
milestones.misconception: Developers think WAL is just 'a better rollback journal' — a drop-in upgrade that's strictly superior. They assume WAL mode has no downsides.
milestones.reveal: WAL fundamentally inverts the durability strategy. The rollback journal writes old pages (undo log) and modifies the database in-place. WAL writes new pages (redo log) to a separate file and leaves the database file untouched. This inversion enables concurrent readers — readers see the database as of their start time (snapshot isolation) because the main database file doesn't change during writes. But WAL introduces new problems: the WAL file grows unboundedly without checkpointing, long-running readers prevent WAL truncation (pinning old frames), checkpoint I/O is bursty (writing potentially thousands of pages at once), and WAL requires shared memory (wal-index) for coordination — which means it doesn't work over network file systems. Each mode has fundamental tradeoffs, not just performance differences.
cascade: Snapshot isolation as MVCC-lite — WAL readers seeing a frozen view of the database is the same concept as PostgreSQL's MVCC (multi-version concurrency control) and Git's snapshots. Every version control system is essentially a WAL (cross-domain: version control / distributed systems)

Append-only as a design pattern — WAL's append-only structure appears in Kafka (commit log), event sourcing (append events, derive state), and even accounting (append journal entries, never modify) (cross-domain: distributed systems / finance)

Checkpoint as compaction — merging WAL into the database is the same operation as LSM-tree compaction in RocksDB, minor/major GC cycles, and even memory defragmentation (cross-domain: systems programming)

Log-structured vs in-place update — this is the fundamental storage engine dichotomy. B-trees update in place (with journaling); LSM-trees are fully log-structured. WAL adds a log to an in-place system, creating a hybrid
yaml_acceptance_criteria: WAL mode appends modified pages to a separate WAL file instead of modifying the main database file
Writers append to WAL; readers check WAL for the most recent version of a page before reading from the main database
Multiple readers can execute queries concurrently while a single writer appends to the WAL
Checkpoint (PRAGMA wal_checkpoint) copies WAL pages back into the main database file
WAL checkpoint is required to prevent unbounded WAL growth—auto-checkpoint triggers after configurable page count (default 1000)
Readers see a consistent snapshot: a reader that starts before a commit does not see that commit's changes (snapshot isolation for reads)
WAL file corruption is detected via page checksums

milestones.title: Aggregate Functions & JOIN
milestones.summary: Implement aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX), GROUP BY with HAVING, and nested loop INNER JOIN execution with correct NULL handling throughout.
milestones.misconception: Developers think GROUP BY just sorts the data and then aggregates run on sorted groups. They assume aggregation is a simple post-processing step after the scan.
milestones.reveal: GROUP BY requires maintaining per-group accumulator state — a hash table mapping group keys to running aggregate values (count, sum, min, max per group). Each row updates its group's accumulators in O(1) rather than requiring a full sort. Sort-based grouping (O(n log n)) is only preferable when the result must be ordered or when memory is constrained. The real complexity is NULL: COUNT(*) counts NULLs but COUNT(col) doesn't. AVG must return REAL even for INTEGER columns. SUM of no rows is NULL, not 0 — but COUNT of no rows is 0, not NULL. These edge cases are where aggregate implementations silently produce wrong answers. Nested loop join adds another dimension: it's O(n×m) and scans the inner table once per outer row, making index existence on the inner table critical.
cascade: Hash aggregation vs sort aggregation — the same tradeoff (memory vs I/O) appears in external sorting, MapReduce's shuffle phase, and Spark's exchange operators (cross-domain: distributed computing)

Join algorithms as a spectrum — nested loop is O(n×m), hash join is O(n+m) with O(n) memory, sort-merge join is O(n log n + m log m). Understanding this spectrum applies to any problem involving set intersection or correlation

Accumulator pattern — maintaining running state across a stream of values is the foundation of stream processing (Flink, Kafka Streams), online algorithms, and even financial running totals (cross-domain: stream processing)

NULL propagation in aggregates — the rules are SQL-standard but counterintuitive. This connects to the broader concept of monad-like propagation where 'unknown' contaminates computations, similar to Maybe/Option in functional programming
yaml_acceptance_criteria: COUNT(*) returns the number of rows; COUNT(col) returns count of non-NULL values
SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX produce correct results over grouped and ungrouped queries
GROUP BY groups rows by specified columns before applying aggregate functions
HAVING filters groups after aggregation
INNER JOIN combines rows from two tables matching a join condition
Nested loop join is implemented as the baseline join algorithm
JOIN with WHERE clause filters correctly after join

diagrams.title: SQLite Engine — Full System Architecture Map
diagrams.description: L0 satellite view showing all major subsystems: SQL Frontend (tokenizer → parser → compiler), VDBE execution engine, Buffer Pool, B-tree/B+tree storage layer, Pager (disk I/O), WAL/Journal. Shows data flow from SQL text input through to disk pages. Every milestone's component is labeled with its anchor ID. This is the 'home base' that all other diagrams reference.

diagrams.title: Data Flow: SQL Statement to Disk Page
diagrams.description: Trace a single INSERT statement from raw SQL text through tokenization, parsing, AST construction, bytecode compilation, VM execution, buffer pool page fetch, B-tree insertion, and final disk write. Shows the exact transformation at each layer boundary.

diagrams.title: Tokenizer Finite State Machine
diagrams.description: State machine diagram showing transitions between states: START, IN_KEYWORD, IN_STRING (with escaped quote handling), IN_NUMBER (integer vs float branch), IN_OPERATOR (single vs multi-char), IN_IDENTIFIER (quoted vs unquoted). Shows how the same character (') has different meanings in different states.

diagrams.title: Token Stream Trace Example
diagrams.description: Data walk showing the character-by-character tokenization of SELECT name, age FROM users WHERE status = 'it''s active' AND id > 42, producing the exact token stream with types, values, and source positions.

diagrams.title: Tokenizer Ambiguity Cases
diagrams.description: Before/after diagram showing ambiguous inputs: minus sign (subtraction vs negative number vs comment start), single quote (string delimiter vs escaped quote), comparison operators (<, <=, <>). Shows how context/lookahead resolves each case.

diagrams.title: SQL Grammar Rule Hierarchy
diagrams.description: Structure layout showing the recursive grammar rules as a call graph: parse_statement → parse_select → parse_column_list, parse_from, parse_where → parse_expression → parse_or → parse_and → parse_comparison → parse_primary. Shows how recursive descent mirrors grammar nesting.

diagrams.title: AST for a Complex SELECT
diagrams.description: Tree diagram showing the full AST for SELECT name, age FROM users WHERE status = 'active' AND age > 25 ORDER BY name. Each node shows its type (SelectStatement, BinaryExpression, LiteralExpression, etc.) and children.

diagrams.title: Operator Precedence Climbing
diagrams.description: Step-by-step state evolution showing how the expression a OR b AND c > 5 is parsed with correct precedence (NOT > comparison > AND > OR). Shows the call stack depth at each step and how the tree is built bottom-up.

diagrams.title: Parser Error Reporting Flow
diagrams.description: Trace showing how the parser handles SELECT * FORM users (typo in FROM), including token position tracking, error message generation, and the expected-vs-actual token comparison.

diagrams.title: VDBE Architecture Overview
diagrams.description: Street-level view of the Virtual Database Engine: program counter, instruction array, register file, cursor array (open B-tree iterators), and result accumulator. Shows how these components interact during execution.

diagrams.title: AST to Bytecode: SELECT Compilation
diagrams.description: Before/after diagram showing a SELECT WHERE AST on the left and the generated bytecode program on the right. Each AST node maps to specific opcodes with arrows showing the correspondence.

diagrams.title: VDBE Execution Trace
diagrams.description: Data walk showing the fetch-decode-execute loop for a 5-instruction program: shows program counter, current opcode, register file state, and cursor position at each step. Includes a conditional jump (WHERE filter) that skips a row.

diagrams.title: Core Opcode Reference
diagrams.description: Structure layout of the key opcodes organized by category: Table ops (OpenTable, Rewind, Next, Close), Data ops (Column, MakeRecord, Insert), Control flow (Goto, If, Halt), Comparison (Eq, Ne, Lt, Gt, Le, Ge), Output (ResultRow). Shows operand format for each.

diagrams.title: Register Allocation for Nested Expressions
diagrams.description: Trace showing how the compiler allocates registers for WHERE (a + b) * c > d - e, demonstrating how intermediate values are stored without clobbering. Shows the register file evolving through the computation.

diagrams.title: Buffer Pool Architecture
diagrams.description: Street-level view showing the buffer pool's internal structure: frame array (fixed-size slots), page table (hash map: page_id → frame_id), LRU list (doubly-linked list of unpinned frames), and connections to the pager/disk layer below and B-tree layer above.

diagrams.title: FetchPage Decision Flow
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing the FetchPage algorithm: check page table → hit? return frame : check free list → free frame? load page : find LRU victim → dirty? write back : load page into frame → update page table → pin → return.

diagrams.title: LRU Eviction State Evolution
diagrams.description: State evolution showing 6 page accesses on a 4-frame buffer pool. Shows the LRU list reordering after each access, a dirty page write-back on eviction, and a pinned page being skipped during eviction. Includes hit/miss annotations.

diagrams.title: Dirty Page Lifecycle
diagrams.description: State evolution diagram showing a page through its lifecycle: clean (loaded from disk) → dirty (modified by B-tree) → write-back (evicted or flushed) → clean (on disk). Shows pin count transitions alongside.

diagrams.title: B-tree vs B+tree: Side-by-Side Comparison
diagrams.description: Before/after (or side-by-side) diagram showing the same 15 key-value pairs stored in a B-tree (data in all nodes) and a B+tree (data only in leaves, linked leaf chain). Highlights fanout difference, leaf linking, and when each is preferable.

diagrams.title: Page Binary Layout (Slotted Page)
diagrams.description: Microscopic byte-level diagram of a 4096-byte page: page header (12 bytes: type, cell count, free space offset, right-child pointer), cell pointer array (growing downward from header), free space in the middle, cell content area (growing upward from page bottom). Shows exact byte offsets.

diagrams.title: Leaf Page vs Internal Page Structure
diagrams.description: Side-by-side diagram showing a table B-tree leaf page (cells contain rowid + full serialized row) and an internal page (cells contain rowid separator + child page number). Shows how the same page format supports both with the type flag.

diagrams.title: Row Serialization Format
diagrams.description: Byte-level diagram showing how a row (id=42, name='Alice', age=30, score=3.14) is serialized: header length (varint), type codes for each column (varint each), then column values in order. Shows varint encoding for the integer 42 (single byte 0x2A) vs a larger number.

diagrams.title: B-tree Node Split on Insert Overflow
diagrams.description: State evolution showing: (1) a full leaf page, (2) a new key arriving, (3) the leaf splitting into two pages, (4) the median key being promoted to the parent internal page. Shows before and after states of both child and parent pages.

diagrams.title: B-tree Traversal for Point Lookup
diagrams.description: Data walk showing a lookup for rowid=73 traversing from root → internal → leaf, showing the binary search within each page's cell pointer array and the comparison logic at each level.

diagrams.title: Variable-Length Integer (Varint) Encoding
diagrams.description: Microscopic diagram showing how integers of different sizes map to 1-9 byte varints: 0-127 (1 byte), 128-16383 (2 bytes), etc. Shows the bit layout with continuation bits. Compares to UTF-8 and Protocol Buffer varint encoding.

diagrams.title: System Catalog (sqlite_master) Structure
diagrams.description: Diagram showing the sqlite_master table stored as the B-tree rooted at page 1, with columns: type, name, tbl_name, rootpage, sql. Shows how CREATE TABLE records the root page number and how the engine bootstraps by reading page 1.

diagrams.title: B-tree Cursor Iterator Pattern
diagrams.description: Street-level diagram showing the cursor abstraction: open(root_page) → rewind(go to leftmost leaf) → read current cell → next(advance to next cell, crossing page boundaries) → close. Shows cursor state (current page, cell index) at each step.

diagrams.title: SELECT Execution End-to-End Trace
diagrams.description: Data walk tracing SELECT name FROM users WHERE age > 25 through: bytecode program → VM opens cursor → rewind to first leaf → for each row: deserialize → check predicate → project column → emit result row → next. Shows which rows pass/fail the WHERE filter.

diagrams.title: Three-Valued Logic Truth Tables
diagrams.description: Truth tables for AND, OR, NOT with three values (TRUE, FALSE, NULL). Highlights the surprising cases: NULL AND TRUE = NULL, NULL OR TRUE = TRUE, NULL = NULL = NULL (not TRUE). Shows how this affects WHERE clause evaluation.

diagrams.title: INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE Write Path
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing the write path for each DML operation: INSERT (serialize row → find leaf → insert cell → maybe split), UPDATE (find row → modify → maybe reposition if key changed), DELETE (find row → mark deleted or remove cell → maybe merge). Shows buffer pool interaction at each step.

diagrams.title: Column Projection from Serialized Record
diagrams.description: Microscopic trace showing how SELECT col2, col4 FROM t deserializes a variable-length record: read header to find column offsets, skip to col2 offset, read col2 value, skip to col4 offset, read col4 value. Shows that non-projected columns are never fully decoded.

diagrams.title: Secondary Index B+tree Structure
diagrams.description: Street-level diagram showing a B+tree index on users.email: internal nodes contain email separators, leaf nodes contain (email, rowid) pairs with linked leaves. Shows the double lookup: index B+tree → rowid → table B-tree → full row.

diagrams.title: Index Lookup vs Full Table Scan
diagrams.description: Before/after comparison: Left shows a full table scan reading all 1000 pages. Right shows an index lookup reading 3 index pages + 1 table page for a single-row result. Includes page-read counters to quantify the difference.

diagrams.title: Index Range Scan via Leaf Traversal
diagrams.description: Data walk showing a range query WHERE price BETWEEN 10 AND 50: descend index B+tree to first leaf with key ≥ 10, then follow leaf links until key > 50. Shows the linked leaf page chain enabling efficient sequential access.

diagrams.title: Index Maintenance on INSERT
diagrams.description: State evolution showing an INSERT that must update both the table B-tree and two secondary index B+trees. Shows the three separate B-tree insertions and the write amplification cost.

diagrams.title: Double Lookup: Index → Table B-tree
diagrams.description: Trace diagram showing the full path: query arrives → traverse index B+tree (3 pages) → extract rowid → traverse table B-tree (3 pages) → extract full row. Shows total I/O cost and why covering indexes eliminate the second traversal.

diagrams.title: Query Planner Decision Flow
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing the planner's decision process: enumerate candidate plans (table scan, each available index) → estimate selectivity per predicate → compute I/O cost per plan → select minimum-cost plan. Shows threshold where index scan becomes worse than table scan.

diagrams.title: Selectivity Estimation with Statistics
diagrams.description: Diagram showing how ANALYZE data (row_count=10000, distinct_values=100 for status column) estimates selectivity: WHERE status = 'active' → selectivity = 1/100 = 1% → estimated_rows = 100. Shows how this translates to I/O cost comparison.

diagrams.title: Cost Model: Table Scan vs Index Scan
diagrams.description: Before/after showing two plans for the same query: table scan (sequential read of 500 pages, cost = 500) vs index scan at 30% selectivity (3000 random reads through index + table, cost = 3000). Demonstrates when table scan wins despite index availability.

diagrams.title: Join Order Optimization
diagrams.description: Diagram showing a 3-table join with two possible orderings: (A ⋈ B) ⋈ C vs (A ⋈ C) ⋈ B. Shows estimated intermediate result sizes for each ordering and how the planner picks the one with smaller intermediate results. Illustrates cardinality error compounding.

diagrams.title: EXPLAIN Output Anatomy
diagrams.description: Annotated example of EXPLAIN output for a query using an index, showing each field: opcode, operands, scan type, index name, estimated rows. Maps each line back to the planner's decision.

diagrams.title: Rollback Journal Write Protocol
diagrams.description: State evolution showing the complete transaction lifecycle: (1) BEGIN — create journal file, (2) before each page write — copy original page to journal, (3) fsync journal, (4) write modified pages to database, (5) COMMIT — delete journal. Shows the exact ordering constraint at each step.

diagrams.title: Crash Recovery: Four Scenarios
diagrams.description: Four-panel diagram showing crash at different points: (A) crash before journal fsync — no journal on disk, database untouched, no recovery needed; (B) crash after journal fsync, before db write — hot journal detected, restore original pages; (C) crash during db write — hot journal detected, restore; (D) crash after journal delete — transaction committed, no recovery needed.

diagrams.title: Rollback Journal File Format
diagrams.description: Byte-level layout of the journal file: header (magic number, page count, database size, sector size, page size), followed by page records (page number + original page content). Shows how recovery reads the journal sequentially.

diagrams.title: Write Ordering: Why fsync Position Matters
diagrams.description: Before/after showing correct ordering (journal fsync → db write → journal delete) vs incorrect ordering (db write → journal fsync). Shows how incorrect ordering leads to unrecoverable corruption: modified database pages with no undo information.

diagrams.title: Torn Page Write Problem
diagrams.description: Microscopic diagram showing a 4KB page write interrupted at byte 2048: top half is new data, bottom half is old data. Shows how the rollback journal's complete original page image enables recovery even from torn writes.

diagrams.title: WAL Mode Architecture
diagrams.description: Street-level view showing the three files: main database (.db), WAL file (.db-wal), and WAL index (.db-shm, shared memory). Shows writers appending to WAL, readers checking WAL-index then falling back to main db, and checkpoint copying WAL pages back.

diagrams.title: WAL vs Rollback Journal: Inverted Strategies
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison: Rollback journal stores OLD pages in journal, modifies database in-place. WAL stores NEW pages in WAL, leaves database untouched. Shows the fundamental inversion and its consequences for concurrency.

diagrams.title: WAL Read Path: Page Version Lookup
diagrams.description: Data walk showing a reader looking for page 42: check WAL-index hash table for page 42 → find most recent frame ≤ reader's start frame → if found, read from WAL → if not, read from main database. Shows snapshot isolation in action.

diagrams.title: Concurrent Readers and Writer in WAL Mode
diagrams.description: Timeline diagram showing three readers and one writer operating concurrently. Each reader has a start-frame marker showing their snapshot boundary. Writer appends new frames. Shows how readers see consistent snapshots without blocking the writer.

diagrams.title: WAL Checkpoint Process
diagrams.description: State evolution showing checkpoint: (1) wait for no readers using old frames, (2) copy modified pages from WAL to main database in page order, (3) reset WAL file. Shows why long-running readers block checkpoint truncation.

diagrams.title: WAL Frame Format
diagrams.description: Byte-level layout of a WAL frame: frame header (page number, database size after commit, salt values, checksum) followed by the full 4KB page content. Shows how checksums detect corruption.

diagrams.title: Aggregate Execution with GROUP BY
diagrams.description: Data walk showing GROUP BY department with COUNT(*) and AVG(salary): hash table maps department → {count, sum} accumulator. Each row updates its group's accumulator. Final pass emits one result row per group.

diagrams.title: NULL Handling in Aggregate Functions
diagrams.description: Table showing a 5-row dataset with NULLs and the result of each aggregate: COUNT(*) = 5, COUNT(col) = 3, SUM = computes only non-NULLs, AVG = SUM/COUNT(col) not SUM/COUNT(*). Shows why COUNT(*) ≠ COUNT(col).

diagrams.title: Nested Loop Join Execution
diagrams.description: Trace showing INNER JOIN between orders (3 rows) and customers (4 rows): outer loop iterates orders, inner loop scans all customers per order row, match condition checked for each pair. Shows the 3×4 = 12 comparisons and which produce output rows.

diagrams.title: GROUP BY → HAVING → Output Pipeline
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing the execution pipeline: scan rows → hash into groups → compute aggregates per group → apply HAVING filter (post-aggregation) → project output columns → emit results. Contrasts WHERE (pre-aggregation) vs HAVING (post-aggregation).

diagrams.title: Nested Loop Join With vs Without Index
diagrams.description: Before/after showing nested loop join on inner table: without index (full scan per outer row, O(n×m) page reads) vs with index on join column (index lookup per outer row, O(n×log m) page reads). Quantifies the I/O difference.

tdd_blueprint.design_vision: A test-first construction of a relational database engine from raw bytes to query optimizer. Each module isolates one architectural layer so tests encode invariants before implementation: FSM correctness, page-layout binary contracts, crash-safety journal ordering, and WAL frame checksums. Domain profile: data-storage / expert — every on-disk structure gets a byte-level layout diagram; every stateful protocol gets a state-machine diagram.

modules.name: SQL Tokenizer
modules.description: Implement a finite-state-machine tokenizer that converts a raw SQL string into a flat token stream. The tokenizer must handle keywords, identifiers, string literals, numeric literals, operators, and whitespace/comment skipping without allocating per-token heap memory for the common path.

specs.title: FSM state transitions on ASCII input
specs.description: Feed single characters into the FSM and assert the resulting state transition. Cover every edge: INITIAL→IDENT on letter, INITIAL→NUMBER on digit, INITIAL→STRING on single-quote, IDENT→IDENT on alphanumeric, IDENT→DONE on non-alphanumeric.
expected_outputs: state transitions matching the FSM table
edge_cases: NUL byte terminates scan
embedded single-quote in string literal requires escape handling
0x7F and above are rejected as non-ASCII

specs.title: Keyword recognition is case-insensitive
specs.description: After scanning an identifier, the tokenizer must perform a case-insensitive lookup against the keyword table. 'SELECT', 'select', 'SeLeCt' must all produce TK_SELECT.
edge_cases: keywords that are prefixes of identifiers (e.g., 'into' vs 'integer')

specs.title: Full SQL statement produces correct token sequence
specs.description: Tokenize 'SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE id = 42;' and assert the complete ordered token list with types and lexeme slices.
inputs: "SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE id = 42;"
expected_outputs: [TK_SELECT, TK_ID('id'), TK_COMMA, TK_ID('name'), TK_FROM, TK_ID('users'), TK_WHERE, TK_ID('id'), TK_EQ, TK_INTEGER(42), TK_SEMI, TK_EOF]
edge_cases: trailing whitespace after semicolon
multiple consecutive spaces

specs.title: Tokenizer returns error token on illegal input
specs.description: Characters outside the grammar (e.g., '@', '`' in non-identifier position, unclosed string literal) must produce TK_ILLEGAL without panicking.
edge_cases: empty string produces only TK_EOF
string with only whitespace produces only TK_EOF

specs.title: Token struct has zero heap allocation for lexeme slice
specs.description: Each Token must be a struct containing (type, start_offset, length) pointing into the original input buffer. No String allocation should occur during tokenization. Verify by asserting token.lexeme() returns a &str slice of the original input.
edge_cases: lexeme at position 0
lexeme spanning to the last byte

implementation_phases.name: Token type enum and struct
implementation_phases.description: Define TokenType enum covering all SQL tokens. Define Token { token_type, start, len }. Implement lexeme(&self, src: &str) -> &str.

implementation_phases.name: FSM core loop
implementation_phases.description: Implement Tokenizer { src, pos }. Write next_token() using a match-based FSM. Handle INITIAL, IDENT, NUMBER, STRING, OPERATOR, WHITESPACE states.

implementation_phases.name: Keyword table lookup
implementation_phases.description: Implement a sorted static keyword table. After scanning an IDENT, perform binary search with case-insensitive compare. Remap TK_ID to the keyword type if found.

implementation_phases.name: Iterator adapter and error handling
implementation_phases.description: Implement Iterator<Item=Token> for Tokenizer. On TK_ILLEGAL, continue iteration (do not panic). Add tokenize_all(src) -> Vec<Token> convenience function.

diagrams.title: Tokenizer FSM State Transitions
diagrams.description: Complete FSM: states INITIAL, IN_IDENT, IN_NUMBER, IN_STRING, IN_LINE_COMMENT, IN_BLOCK_COMMENT, DONE, ERROR. Transitions labeled with character class (letter, digit, quote, slash-star, newline, other, NUL). Accept states and emitted token type shown per terminal transition.

diagrams.title: Token Struct Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Byte-level layout of Token struct: [0..1] token_type (u8), [1..3] pad, [4..7] start (u32), [8..11] len (u32). Annotate that no heap pointer is stored; lexeme is computed by slicing original &str. Total size: 12 bytes.

diagrams.title: Tokenizer Data Flow
diagrams.description: Input SQL string bytes → FSM character consumer → token emit queue → caller's token stream. Show the zero-copy path: lexeme slice points into original buffer. Show keyword table lookup as a side-channel after IDENT recognition.

modules.name: Recursive-Descent Parser and AST
modules.description: Build a hand-written recursive-descent parser that consumes the token stream and produces a typed AST. The parser must handle SELECT (with projections, WHERE, ORDER BY, LIMIT), INSERT, CREATE TABLE, and DROP TABLE. Expression parsing must implement Pratt precedence for arithmetic and comparison operators.

specs.title: Parse simple SELECT into correct AST shape
specs.description: Parse 'SELECT a, b FROM t' and assert the resulting SelectStmt node contains projection list [ColRef('a'), ColRef('b')], from = TableRef('t'), where_clause = None.
expected_outputs: SelectStmt { projections: [ColRef('a'), ColRef('b')], from: TableRef('t'), where_clause: None, order_by: [], limit: None }
edge_cases: SELECT * FROM t produces Projection::Star
aliased column: SELECT a AS alias

specs.title: WHERE clause expression tree respects operator precedence
specs.description: Parse 'SELECT x FROM t WHERE a + b * c = 10' and assert the expression tree is Eq(Add(ColRef('a'), Mul(ColRef('b'), ColRef('c'))), Lit(10)). Multiplication must bind tighter than addition.
inputs: "SELECT x FROM t WHERE a + b * c = 10"
expected_outputs: BinOp(Eq, BinOp(Add, ColRef(a), BinOp(Mul, ColRef(b), ColRef(c))), Lit(10))
edge_cases: parenthesized expressions override precedence
unary minus: WHERE -x = 0

specs.title: CREATE TABLE produces column definition list with types
specs.description: Parse 'CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL)' and assert column defs with correct types and constraints.
inputs: "CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL)"
expected_outputs: CreateTableStmt { name: 'users', columns: [ColDef('id', INTEGER, [PrimaryKey]), ColDef('name', TEXT, [NotNull])] }
edge_cases: table with single column
column with no constraints

specs.title: Syntax errors produce ParseError with position
specs.description: Feed malformed SQL and assert that parse() returns Err(ParseError { position, message }). Position must point to the offending token.
edge_cases: empty input returns ParseError
trailing garbage after valid statement

specs.title: AST round-trip: parse then unparse matches normalized form
specs.description: Parse a SQL string, call ast.to_sql(), then parse again. The two ASTs must be structurally equal. Tests the Display impl on AST nodes.
inputs: "SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE id > 5 ORDER BY name LIMIT 10"
edge_cases: INSERT with VALUES clause
nested expressions

implementation_phases.name: AST node types
implementation_phases.description: Define Stmt enum (Select, Insert, CreateTable, DropTable). Define Expr enum (Lit, ColRef, BinOp, UnaryOp). Define SelectStmt, InsertStmt, CreateTableStmt structs with all fields.

implementation_phases.name: Parser struct and token consumption helpers
implementation_phases.description: Implement Parser { tokens: &[Token], pos: usize, src: &str }. Implement peek(), advance(), expect(type), match_token(type) -> bool.

implementation_phases.name: Pratt expression parser
implementation_phases.description: Implement parse_expr(min_bp: u8) -> Expr. Define binding-power table for all operators. Handle prefix (unary minus, NOT), infix (arithmetic, comparison, AND/OR), and parenthesized groups.

implementation_phases.name: Statement parsers
implementation_phases.description: Implement parse_select(), parse_insert(), parse_create_table(), parse_drop_table(). Dispatch from parse_statement() based on first keyword.

diagrams.title: AST Node Type Hierarchy
diagrams.description: Class/enum diagram of the AST. Stmt enum variants pointing to SelectStmt, InsertStmt, CreateTableStmt. SelectStmt has projections: Vec<Projection>, from: TableRef, where_clause: Option<Expr>, order_by: Vec<OrderTerm>, limit: Option<Expr>. Expr enum variants: Lit(Value), ColRef(String), BinOp(Op, Box<Expr>, Box<Expr>), UnaryOp(Op, Box<Expr>).

diagrams.title: Pratt Precedence Climbing Algorithm
diagrams.description: Step-by-step trace of parse_expr(0) on 'a + b * c': (1) parse primary 'a', (2) peek '+' has left-bp=4 ≥ min_bp=0 → consume, (3) recursive call parse_expr(5) → parses 'b*c' (right-bp of + is 5), (4) inside: parse 'b', peek '*' has left-bp=6 ≥ 5 → consume, (5) parse_expr(7) → parse 'c', (6) return Mul(b,c), (7) return Add(a, Mul(b,c)). Binding power table shown alongside.

diagrams.title: Parser Token Consumption Sequence
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram: caller → parse_statement() → peek TK_SELECT → parse_select() → expect(FROM) → parse_table_ref() → optional parse_where() → parse_expr(0) → return SelectStmt. Show error path: expect() fails → ParseError propagated upward.

modules.name: Bytecode Compiler (VDBE)
modules.description: Implement the Virtual Database Engine bytecode compiler and interpreter. The compiler walks the AST and emits a linear sequence of opcodes targeting a register-based VM. The interpreter executes opcodes against a register file and a cursor abstraction over table data.

specs.title: Compile SELECT column list to OP_Column + OP_ResultRow opcodes
specs.description: Compile 'SELECT a, b FROM t' and assert the opcode sequence contains: OP_OpenRead(cursor=0, table='t'), loop body with OP_Column(cursor=0, col=0, reg=1), OP_Column(cursor=0, col=1, reg=2), OP_ResultRow(reg=1, count=2), OP_Next(cursor=0), OP_Halt.
inputs: SelectStmt for 'SELECT a, b FROM t'
edge_cases: SELECT * expands to all columns at compile time
column alias does not affect opcode generation

specs.title: Arithmetic expression compiles to register operations
specs.description: Compile expression 'a + b * 2' and assert the opcode sequence: OP_Column(cursor, col_b, r1), OP_Integer(2, r2), OP_Multiply(r1, r2, r3), OP_Column(cursor, col_a, r4), OP_Add(r4, r3, r5). Post-order emission.
expected_outputs: opcodes in post-order with correct register assignments
edge_cases: constant folding: Lit(2)+Lit(3) emits OP_Integer(5) directly
division by literal zero emits OP_Halt with SQLITE_ERROR

specs.title: WHERE clause compiles to conditional jump
specs.description: Compile WHERE id = 42 and assert OP_Column(cursor, id_col, r1), OP_Integer(42, r2), OP_Ne(r1, r2, jump_to_next) is emitted, where jump_to_next points to the OP_Next instruction.
expected_outputs: OP_Ne with correct jump target after backpatching
edge_cases: AND condition: two sequential OP_Ne jumps
IS NULL: OP_IsNull opcode

specs.title: VM executes opcode sequence on mock cursor
specs.description: Provide a mock cursor that returns three rows [(1,'alice'),(2,'bob'),(3,'carol')]. Execute OP_OpenRead → loop → OP_ResultRow. Collect results and assert three rows returned in order.
inputs: compiled program + mock cursor with 3 rows
edge_cases: empty table returns zero rows
cursor positioned past last row returns SQLITE_DONE

specs.title: Register file bounds: accessing unallocated register panics in debug
specs.description: Accessing a register index ≥ num_registers must panic in debug mode and return Value::Null in release mode. Assert the panic occurs in cfg(debug_assertions).
expected_outputs: panic in debug, Value::Null in release
edge_cases: register index = usize::MAX

implementation_phases.name: Opcode enum and Instruction struct
implementation_phases.description: Define Opcode enum: OpenRead, Column, ResultRow, Next, Halt, Integer, String, Add, Subtract, Multiply, Divide, Eq, Ne, Lt, Gt, IsNull, Jump, If, IfNot. Define Instruction { opcode, p1: i32, p2: i32, p3: i32, p4: String }.

implementation_phases.name: Compiler: expression emission with register allocator
implementation_phases.description: Implement Compiler { program: Vec<Instruction>, next_reg: usize }. Implement compile_expr(expr) -> RegisterId using post-order traversal. Implement alloc_reg() and free_reg(r).

implementation_phases.name: Compiler: statement emission with backpatching
implementation_phases.description: Implement compile_select(stmt). Emit loop scaffold: OP_OpenRead, label loop_top, compile WHERE with jump (hole), compile projections, OP_ResultRow, OP_Next(loop_top), OP_Halt. Backpatch the WHERE jump hole.

implementation_phases.name: VM interpreter
implementation_phases.description: Implement VM { program, registers: Vec<Value>, cursors: Vec<Box<dyn Cursor>>, pc: usize }. Implement step() -> StepResult. Implement run() -> Vec<Row> calling step() until DONE or HALT.

diagrams.title: VDBE Opcode Catalog and Register Flow
diagrams.description: Box diagram of VDBE VM. Central register file (array of Value). Opcode categories: data load (Column, Integer, String), arithmetic (Add, Subtract, Multiply, Divide), comparison (Eq, Ne, Lt, Gt, IsNull), control flow (Jump, If, Next, Halt), I/O (OpenRead, ResultRow). Arrows show which opcodes read/write which register slots.

diagrams.title: Register File Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Byte layout of Value enum (tagged union): [0] tag byte (0=Null,1=Int,2=Real,3=Text,4=Blob), [1..3] pad, [4..11] payload (i64 for Int, f64 for Real, (ptr,len) for Text/Blob). Total: 24 bytes. Show register file as contiguous array: reg[0..N-1] each 24 bytes. Annotate alignment requirements.

diagrams.title: AST-to-Bytecode Compilation Sequence
diagrams.description: Sequence: Compiler.compile_select(stmt) → emit OP_OpenRead → for each projection: compile_expr → alloc_reg → emit OP_Column → after all: emit OP_ResultRow(first_reg, count) → emit OP_Next(loop_top) → emit OP_Halt. Show backpatching step: WHERE jump hole filled after loop_top label is known.

diagrams.title: VM Execution Trace for SELECT with WHERE
diagrams.description: Trace execution of 'SELECT name FROM users WHERE id > 2' on 3-row table. Instruction-by-instruction: pc=0 OP_OpenRead → pc=1 OP_Column(id,r1) → pc=2 OP_Integer(2,r2) → pc=3 OP_Le(r1,r2,jump=8) [row 1: id=1, jump taken] → pc=8 OP_Next → pc=1 [row 2: id=2, jump taken] → pc=1 [row 3: id=3, fall through] → pc=4 OP_Column(name,r3) → pc=5 OP_ResultRow(r3,1) → emit 'carol' → pc=6 OP_Next → DONE → pc=7 OP_Halt.

modules.name: Buffer Pool Manager
modules.description: Implement a fixed-size buffer pool that manages a pool of page-sized frames in memory. The pool must implement LRU eviction, dirty page tracking, and a pin/unpin reference counting discipline. All disk I/O must be mediated through the pool — no code outside the pool may call read/write on the database file.

specs.title: fetch_page returns correct data for cached page
specs.description: Pre-populate frame 0 with page_id=5 data. Call fetch_page(5). Assert returned frame pointer equals frame 0 and pin count increments to 1. No disk I/O should occur.
edge_cases: two concurrent fetches of same page: pin_count = 2

specs.title: fetch_page triggers disk read on cache miss
specs.description: Pool is empty. Call fetch_page(7) with a mock disk that returns known bytes. Assert frame is populated with those bytes, pin_count=1, and mock disk read_count=1.
inputs: page_id=7, pool empty, mock disk returns [0xAB * PAGE_SIZE]
edge_cases: disk read returns IO error → fetch_page returns Err

specs.title: LRU eviction selects correct victim frame
specs.description: Fill a 4-frame pool with pages 1,2,3,4 (all unpinned). Access pages in order 1,3,2 (making 4 the LRU). Request page 5. Assert page 4 is evicted (written to disk if dirty). Assert page 5 is loaded into the formerly-page-4 frame.
inputs: 4-frame pool, pages 1-4 loaded, access order 1,3,2, request page 5
edge_cases: all frames pinned → fetch_page returns Err(BufferPoolFull)
LRU tie broken by frame index

specs.title: Dirty page is written to disk on eviction
specs.description: Load page 2 into a frame. Mark it dirty. Trigger eviction of page 2. Assert disk.write_count=1 and written bytes match frame contents. Clean page eviction asserts write_count=0.
inputs: page 2 loaded, marked dirty, eviction triggered
edge_cases: write error during eviction → Err propagated, frame remains pinned

specs.title: unpin decrements pin count; zero pin allows eviction
specs.description: Fetch page 3 twice (pin_count=2). Unpin once (pin_count=1). Assert page 3 cannot be evicted. Unpin again (pin_count=0). Assert page 3 is now in the LRU eviction set.
edge_cases: unpin with pin_count=0 panics in debug mode

implementation_phases.name: Frame struct and pool array
implementation_phases.description: Define Frame { data: [u8; PAGE_SIZE], page_id: Option<PageId>, pin_count: u32, is_dirty: bool }. Define BufferPool { frames: [Frame; POOL_SIZE], page_table: HashMap<PageId,FrameId>, lru_list: VecDeque<FrameId> }.

implementation_phases.name: LRU list management
implementation_phases.description: Implement access(frame_id) to move frame to MRU end of lru_list. Implement find_victim() -> Option<FrameId> that walks lru_list from LRU end, skipping pinned frames.

implementation_phases.name: fetch_page and unpin_page
implementation_phases.description: Implement fetch_page(page_id) -> Result<FrameId>: check page_table, on hit call access(), increment pin_count. On miss, call find_victim(), evict (write if dirty), read from disk, update page_table. Implement unpin_page(frame_id, is_dirty).

implementation_phases.name: flush_all and crash-safety guarantee
implementation_phases.description: Implement flush_all() -> Result<()>: write all dirty frames to disk in page_id order. Must be called before journal operations to enforce write ordering.

diagrams.title: Buffer Pool Frame Array Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Byte-level layout of Frame struct: [0..4095] data (PAGE_SIZE=4096 bytes), [4096..4099] page_id (u32, 0xFFFFFFFF = empty), [4100] pin_count (u8), [4101] is_dirty (u8), [4102..4103] pad. Total frame size: 4104 bytes. Show pool as contiguous array frames[0..N-1] at base address. Annotate alignment to 4096-byte boundary requirement for O_DIRECT I/O.

diagrams.title: LRU State Machine
diagrams.description: Frame states: EMPTY (no page loaded), CLEAN_PINNED (loaded, pin_count>0, not dirty), DIRTY_PINNED, CLEAN_UNPINNED (evictable), DIRTY_UNPINNED (evictable, must write). Transitions: load→CLEAN_PINNED, pin→CLEAN_PINNED/DIRTY_PINNED, write→DIRTY_*, unpin→CLEAN_UNPINNED/DIRTY_UNPINNED, evict_clean→EMPTY, evict_dirty→(disk write)→EMPTY.

diagrams.title: fetch_page Flow (Cache Miss Path)
diagrams.description: Sequence: caller → fetch_page(7) → check page_table [miss] → find_victim() → check pin_count [all unpinned] → select LRU frame → is_dirty? [yes] → disk.write(old_page_id, frame.data) → disk.read(7, frame.data) → update page_table[7]=frame_id, del page_table[old_id] → increment pin_count → access(frame_id) → return FrameId. Show error branch: all frames pinned → Err(BufferPoolFull).

diagrams.title: Dirty Page Tracking and Flush Data Flow
diagrams.description: Data flow for write path: VM calls write_page(frame_id, offset, bytes) → sets frame.is_dirty=true → does NOT write to disk immediately. On eviction or flush_all(): dirty frames sorted by page_id → sequential disk writes → is_dirty cleared. Show how the buffer pool is the single choke-point between VM and disk.

modules.name: B-tree Page Format and Table Storage
modules.description: Implement SQLite's slotted-page B-tree format on disk. Each 4096-byte page contains a page header, a cell pointer array, and cells stored from the high end growing down. Implement page initialization, cell insertion with overflow detection, and B-tree node split when a page is full.

specs.title: Fresh leaf page has correct binary header
specs.description: Call BTreePage::new_leaf(page_id=1). Serialize to bytes. Assert byte 0 = 0x0D (leaf type), bytes 1-2 = first_freeblock (0x0000 = no freeblocks), bytes 3-4 = cell_count (0x0000), bytes 5-6 = cell_content_area_start (0x1000 = 4096 for fresh page), byte 7 = fragmented_free_bytes (0x00).
edge_cases: interior page has type byte 0x05
page 1 (first page) has 100-byte database header prepended

specs.title: Insert cell updates pointer array and content area
specs.description: Insert a 20-byte cell into a fresh leaf page. Assert: cell_count incremented to 1, cell_content_area_start decremented by 20, cell pointer array[0] = new cell_content_area_start value, cell bytes at that offset match inserted cell.
edge_cases: insert until page full triggers overflow detection
cell pointer array is kept sorted by key

specs.title: Cell binary format: varint key followed by payload bytes
specs.description: Encode a cell with rowid=300 (requires 2-byte varint: 0x82 0x2C) and payload=[0x01,0x02,0x03]. Assert serialized bytes = [0x82, 0x2C, 0x01, 0x02, 0x03]. Then decode back and assert rowid=300, payload=[0x01,0x02,0x03].
edge_cases: rowid=127 encodes as 1 byte
rowid=2^63-1 encodes as 9 bytes
empty payload is valid

specs.title: Node split produces two balanced pages and correct separator key
specs.description: Fill a leaf page to capacity (N cells). Insert one more cell. Assert split is triggered: two new pages each with ~N/2 cells. Assert separator key promoted to parent equals first key of right page. Assert all original keys appear across the two pages with no duplicates.
expected_outputs: left_page.cell_count ≈ N/2, right_page.cell_count ≈ N/2, separator_key = right_page.min_key, left.keys ∪ right.keys = original.keys
edge_cases: split of root creates new root with two children
split with median cell larger than PAGE_SIZE/2 — overflow cell goes to right page

specs.title: Cursor traversal visits all cells in key order
specs.description: Build a B-tree with 100 sequential rowid insertions. Create a cursor at first(). Call next() 99 times. Assert each visited rowid is exactly 1 greater than the previous. Assert 100th next() returns DONE.
edge_cases: cursor on empty tree returns DONE immediately
cursor seek(50) positions before first cell ≥ 50

implementation_phases.name: Page header encode/decode
implementation_phases.description: Implement PageHeader { page_type, first_freeblock, cell_count, cell_content_area, fragmented_free_bytes }. Implement encode(&self, buf: &mut [u8]) and decode(buf: &[u8]) -> PageHeader reading at the offsets defined by SQLite spec.

implementation_phases.name: Varint encode/decode
implementation_phases.description: Implement encode_varint(v: u64, buf: &mut [u8]) -> usize and decode_varint(buf: &[u8]) -> (u64, usize). Handle 1-9 byte encoding. Test with boundary values 127, 128, 16383, 2097151, 2^63-1.

implementation_phases.name: Cell insertion and pointer array management
