implementation_phases.description: Implement BTreePage::insert_cell(key, payload). Allocate space from content area top. Write cell bytes. Insert pointer into sorted pointer array using binary search on key. Update header.

implementation_phases.name: Node split algorithm
implementation_phases.description: Implement split_leaf(overflow_cell) -> (left_page, right_page, separator_key). Collect all cells + overflow into sorted array. Divide at median. Write two new pages via buffer pool. Return separator for parent insertion (may recurse).

implementation_phases.name: Cursor: first, next, seek
implementation_phases.description: Implement BTreeCursor { page_stack: Vec<(FrameId, cell_index)> }. Implement first() to descend to leftmost leaf. Implement next() to advance within leaf, ascending on exhaustion. Implement seek(key) using binary search on pointer arrays at each level.

diagrams.title: B-tree Leaf Page Binary Layout
diagrams.description: Exact byte-offset layout of a 4096-byte leaf page. [0] page_type (0x0D), [1..2] first_freeblock (u16 BE), [3..4] cell_count (u16 BE), [5..6] cell_content_area_start (u16 BE), [7] fragmented_free_bytes. [8..8+2*N-1] cell pointer array (N u16 BE offsets, sorted by key). [gap: unallocated]. [cell_content_area_start..4095] cell data growing downward. Annotate usable bytes = 4096 - 8 - 2*N.

diagrams.title: Cell Binary Format with Varint Key
diagrams.description: Cell wire format: [0..k-1] rowid as varint (k=1..9 bytes), [k..k+p-1] record payload, [k+p..k+p+3] overflow_page_number (u32, present only if payload overflows). Show varint encoding rules: MSB=1 means more bytes follow; 7 bits per byte. Example: rowid=300 → [0x82, 0x2C] (2 bytes). Annotate record payload internal format: header_size varint, then type codes varints, then data.

diagrams.title: Leaf vs Interior Node Structure
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison. Leaf (0x0D): cells contain (rowid, full_record). Interior (0x05): cells contain (left_child_page_ptr u32, separator_key varint); rightmost_child_ptr in header. Show a 3-level tree: root interior → two interior → four leaf pages. Annotate that cursors always descend to leaves.

diagrams.title: Node Split Algorithm Steps
diagrams.description: Step-by-step split of full leaf: (1) Collect all N+1 cells (N existing + 1 overflow) into sorted array. (2) Find median index m = (N+1)/2. (3) Allocate left_page: insert cells[0..m-1]. (4) Allocate right_page: insert cells[m+1..N]. (5) separator_key = cells[m].key (for leaf: copy key; cell[m] goes to right page in leaf splits). (6) Insert separator_key into parent at position p. (7) If parent now full → recurse split_interior(parent). Show tree height increase when root splits.

diagrams.title: Cursor Traversal Data Flow
diagrams.description: Data flow of seek(key=50) then repeated next() calls. BTreeCursor holds page_stack [(root, ptr=1), (interior, ptr=2), (leaf, cell=5)]. On next(): increment cell index → if cell_index ≥ cell_count: pop stack, advance parent → if stack empty: return DONE. Show buffer pool interaction: each page access goes through fetch_page(page_id).

modules.name: SELECT Execution and DML
modules.description: Wire the full read path: VM cursor opcodes driving the B-tree cursor through the buffer pool. Implement table scan, point lookup by rowid, and DML operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) that modify B-tree pages through the buffer pool and mark frames dirty.

specs.title: Full table scan returns all rows in rowid order
specs.description: Create a table with 5 rows. Execute 'SELECT * FROM t'. Assert 5 rows returned, rowids 1-5 in ascending order. Assert each row's column values match inserted values.
expected_outputs: 5 rows, rowid order, correct column values
edge_cases: empty table returns 0 rows
single-row table returns 1 row

specs.title: WHERE id=N uses point lookup, not full scan
specs.description: With a 1000-row table, execute 'SELECT name FROM users WHERE rowid=500'. Assert cursor.seek_count=1 (one seek, no sequential scans). Assert correct row returned.
expected_outputs: exactly 1 row returned, cursor performed 1 seek
edge_cases: rowid not found returns 0 rows
rowid=1 (first row), rowid=1000 (last row)

specs.title: INSERT auto-assigns rowid and persists cell
specs.description: On an empty table, INSERT INTO t VALUES('a'). Assert returned rowid=1. INSERT again VALUES('b'), assert rowid=2. Re-open table and scan: assert 2 rows in order.
expected_outputs: rowids 1 and 2, both rows readable after re-open
edge_cases: INSERT with explicit rowid
INSERT with explicit rowid=MAX_ROWID leaves table unable to auto-assign

specs.title: DELETE removes cell and UPDATE overwrites cell payload
specs.description: Insert rows 1-5. DELETE WHERE rowid=3. Assert scan returns rows 1,2,4,5. UPDATE SET name='X' WHERE rowid=2. Assert scan shows rowid=2 with name='X'.
expected_outputs: 4 rows after delete: [1,2,4,5], name at rowid=2 = 'X'
edge_cases: DELETE non-existent rowid is no-op (0 rows affected)
UPDATE produces overflow if new value larger than old

specs.title: Three-valued logic: NULL comparisons return NULL, not false
specs.description: Execute 'SELECT * FROM t WHERE col = NULL'. Assert 0 rows returned (NULL = NULL is NULL, not TRUE). Execute 'SELECT * FROM t WHERE col IS NULL' on a table with one NULL row. Assert 1 row returned.
inputs: table with NULL in col, WHERE col = NULL vs WHERE col IS NULL
edge_cases: NULL in AND/OR: NULL AND TRUE = NULL, NULL OR TRUE = TRUE
ORDER BY places NULLs first by default

implementation_phases.name: TableCursor wrapping BTreeCursor
implementation_phases.description: Implement TableCursor implementing the Cursor trait: first(), next(), seek(rowid), current_rowid(), read_column(idx) -> Value, write_record(rowid, cols), delete_current().

implementation_phases.name: Record format encoder/decoder
implementation_phases.description: Implement encode_record(cols: &[Value]) -> Vec<u8>: header = (header_len varint, type_code varints), data = serialized column values. Implement decode_record(bytes, col_idx) -> Value for projection without full decode.

implementation_phases.name: DML: INSERT with max-rowid tracking
implementation_phases.description: Implement table.insert(cols) -> RowId: fetch max_rowid (cached in schema), increment, encode_record, call btree.insert(rowid, record). Update max_rowid cache.

implementation_phases.name: DML: UPDATE and DELETE
implementation_phases.description: Implement UPDATE: seek cursor to rowid, encode new record, call btree.update(rowid, new_record) — delete old cell + insert new cell. Implement DELETE: seek cursor, call btree.delete(rowid), handle cell removal and page rebalancing.

diagrams.title: Full Table Scan Loop Sequence
diagrams.description: Sequence: VM OP_OpenRead → TableCursor.first() → BTreeCursor descend to leftmost leaf via buffer pool. Loop: VM OP_Column(col_idx, reg) → cursor.read_column(idx) → decode_record(frame.data, idx) → return Value. VM OP_ResultRow → emit row. VM OP_Next → cursor.next() → advance cell_index or page. OP_Next returns DONE → VM OP_Halt.

diagrams.title: DML Write Path
diagrams.description: INSERT data flow: SQL → Parser → AST InsertStmt → Compiler → OP_Insert opcode → VM → TableCursor.insert(rowid, record) → encode_record → BTreePage.insert_cell → BufferPool marks frame dirty → dirty frame flushed to disk on eviction or flush_all(). Show how all writes are buffered — never bypass pool.

diagrams.title: Three-Valued Logic Truth Table
diagrams.description: Truth tables for AND, OR, NOT with NULL. Show: NULL AND TRUE = NULL, NULL AND FALSE = FALSE, NULL OR TRUE = TRUE, NULL OR FALSE = NULL, NOT NULL = NULL. Show SQL comparison: col = NULL always evaluates to NULL → WHERE clause discards rows where condition is NULL (not TRUE). Show IS NULL opcode: OP_IsNull(r, jump) takes NULL→jump, non-NULL→fall through.

modules.name: Secondary Indexes
modules.description: Implement secondary (non-primary) B-tree indexes. An index B-tree stores (indexed_column_value, rowid) as its key with an empty payload. Implement index creation (CREATE INDEX), index-based lookup (seek to key, collect rowids, double-lookup in table B-tree), and index maintenance on DML operations.

specs.title: Index B-tree stores (key, rowid) cells in sorted order
specs.description: Create an index on column 'name'. Insert entries: ('bob',2), ('alice',1), ('carol',3). Assert index cursor traversal returns entries in order: ('alice',1), ('bob',2), ('carol',3).
edge_cases: duplicate key values: ('alice',1) and ('alice',5) both stored, ordered by (key,rowid)
NULL keys sort before all non-NULL values

specs.title: Index lookup returns correct rowid(s) for exact key match
specs.description: Index on 'name'. Seek to key='bob'. Collect all rowids while current_key='bob'. Assert rowids=[2]. Then double-lookup: table.seek(rowid=2), assert correct record returned.
expected_outputs: rowids=[2], table record at rowid=2 correct
edge_cases: key not in index returns empty set
duplicate key returns all rowids

specs.title: CREATE INDEX populates index from existing table data
specs.description: Insert 10 rows into table. Then execute CREATE INDEX idx_name ON t(name). Assert index contains exactly 10 entries. Assert index traversal matches sorted order of name column.
expected_outputs: index.entry_count=10, entries in sorted name order
edge_cases: CREATE INDEX on empty table creates empty index B-tree
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS is idempotent

specs.title: DML operations maintain index consistency
specs.description: Index on name. INSERT ('dave', rowid=4). Assert index contains ('dave',4). UPDATE rowid=4 SET name='david'. Assert index no longer contains ('dave',4) but contains ('david',4). DELETE rowid=4. Assert ('david',4) removed from index.
inputs: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE on indexed table
expected_outputs: index reflects all mutations, no stale entries
edge_cases: UPDATE of non-indexed column does not touch index
UPDATE of indexed column with no value change is no-op for index

specs.title: Index scan with range predicate (name > 'b') returns correct rows
specs.description: Index on name. Table has: alice, bob, carol, dave. Execute WHERE name > 'b'. Assert index seek to first key > 'b', collect until end: bob, carol, dave. Double-lookup each. Assert 3 rows returned.
edge_cases: range predicate with both lower and upper bound
LIKE 'b%' as range: seek 'b', stop at 'c'

implementation_phases.name: Index key encoding with type comparison
implementation_phases.description: Implement encode_index_key(value: Value, rowid: RowId) -> Vec<u8> using SQLite's record comparison serialization. Implement compare_index_keys(a, b) -> Ordering that respects type affinity rules (NULL < INTEGER/REAL < TEXT < BLOB).

implementation_phases.name: IndexCursor seek and range scan
implementation_phases.description: Implement IndexCursor { btree: BTreeCursor }. Implement seek(key: Value) to position at first entry ≥ key. Implement next() and current_entry() -> (Value, RowId). Implement scan_equal(key) -> Vec<RowId> collecting while current_key == key.

implementation_phases.name: Index maintenance in DML
implementation_phases.description: Modify Table.insert/update/delete to also update all associated indexes. Insert: add (new_value, rowid) to each index. Delete: remove (old_value, rowid) from each index. Update: remove old index entry, insert new entry (only for indexed columns that changed).

implementation_phases.name: CREATE INDEX implementation
implementation_phases.description: Implement execute_create_index(stmt): allocate new B-tree page, scan table with full cursor, for each row encode index key and insert into index B-tree. Register index in schema table.

diagrams.title: Index B-tree and Table B-tree Dual Structure
diagrams.description: Side-by-side: Table B-tree (keyed by rowid, values = full records) and Index B-tree (keyed by (col_value, rowid), values = empty). Show index leaf cells: [col_value_encoded | rowid_varint]. Annotate that index does NOT store the full record — only the lookup key. Show schema table pointing to both B-tree root pages.

diagrams.title: Index Lookup Double-Seek Sequence
diagrams.description: Sequence for WHERE name='bob': (1) Planner selects index. (2) VM OP_OpenRead(index_cursor). (3) OP_SeekGE(index_cursor, 'bob'). (4) Loop: OP_IdxKey(index_cursor, r1) → compare r1='bob' → if ≠ break. (5) OP_IdxRowid(index_cursor, r2) → get rowid. (6) OP_OpenRead(table_cursor). (7) OP_SeekRowid(table_cursor, r2). (8) OP_Column(table_cursor, col, r3) → result. (9) OP_Next(index_cursor) → loop.

diagrams.title: Index Maintenance on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE
diagrams.description: Data flow: DML operation arrives → Table.mutate(). Branches: INSERT → encode_record → table btree insert → for each index: encode_index_key(new_val, rowid) → index btree insert. DELETE → table btree delete → for each index: remove (old_val, rowid). UPDATE → decode old record → table btree update → for each changed indexed column: remove old index entry, add new index entry. Show that index updates are in the same transaction as table updates.

modules.name: Query Planner and Statistics
modules.description: Implement a cost-based query planner that chooses between full table scan and index scan based on estimated row counts and selectivity. Maintain column statistics (min, max, distinct count, null fraction) to drive cost estimation. Implement the plan selection decision for single-table queries and a dynamic-programming join ordering for two-table joins.

specs.title: Cost model prefers index scan for high-selectivity predicate
specs.description: Table has 10000 rows. Index on id. Predicate: id=500 (selectivity ~0.0001). Assert planner selects IndexScan(idx_id) over TableScan. Cost(IndexScan) = log2(10000) * 1.0 ≈ 13.3. Cost(TableScan) = 10000.0. Assert plan.node_type == PlanNode::IndexScan.
inputs: stats: table_rows=10000, predicate id=500, index on id
edge_cases: no index available → always TableScan
selectivity = 1.0 (no WHERE) → TableScan cheaper

specs.title: Cost model prefers table scan for low-selectivity predicate
specs.description: Table has 1000 rows. Index on status. Predicate: status='active' (50% of rows, selectivity=0.5). Cost(IndexScan) = log2(1000) + 500 = 510. Cost(TableScan) = 1000. Assert planner still selects IndexScan but verify that at selectivity > 0.3 threshold the planner switches to TableScan.
expected_outputs: high selectivity: IndexScan; low selectivity: TableScan
edge_cases: equal cost: prefer TableScan (no double lookup overhead)
statistics missing: assume uniform distribution

specs.title: Statistics collection: ANALYZE updates column stats
specs.description: Insert 100 rows with id 1-100, name alternating 'alice'/'bob'. Execute ANALYZE. Assert sqlite_stat1 or equivalent stats table shows: id min=1, max=100, distinct=100; name distinct=2.
edge_cases: ANALYZE on empty table stores zeros
re-running ANALYZE updates stale stats

specs.title: Selectivity estimation uses distinct count
specs.description: Column 'status' has distinct=4 (values: active, inactive, pending, banned). Estimate selectivity for status='active'. Assert estimated_selectivity = 1/4 = 0.25. For a range predicate status > 'inactive', assert estimated rows = table_rows * 0.5 (half the distinct values).
edge_cases: NULL fraction affects row count estimate
predicate on non-indexed column uses column stats only

specs.title: Two-table join ordering: DP selects smaller outer table
specs.description: Tables: orders (rows=10000), customers (rows=500). Join: orders.customer_id = customers.id. Assert DP ordering selects customers as outer loop (smaller), orders as inner (larger). Assert plan = NestedLoopJoin(outer=customers, inner=orders, predicate=customer_id=id).
edge_cases: equal table sizes: either order acceptable
3-table join: DP evaluates all 6 orderings

implementation_phases.name: Statistics structs and storage
implementation_phases.description: Define ColumnStats { min: Value, max: Value, distinct_count: u64, null_fraction: f64, row_count: u64 }. Define TableStats { table_name, row_count, columns: HashMap<String,ColumnStats> }. Store in sqlite_stat1 table. Implement load_stats() and save_stats().

implementation_phases.name: Selectivity estimation
implementation_phases.description: Implement estimate_selectivity(predicate: &Expr, stats: &TableStats) -> f64. Handle eq (1/distinct), range (0.3 heuristic if no histogram), IS NULL (null_fraction), AND (product), OR (1 - product of negations).

implementation_phases.name: Cost model and plan node selection
implementation_phases.description: Implement cost_table_scan(stats) = stats.row_count as f64. Implement cost_index_scan(stats, selectivity, index_height) = index_height + selectivity * stats.row_count. Implement choose_access_path(table, predicates, available_indexes) -> PlanNode.

implementation_phases.name: DP join ordering for N tables
implementation_phases.description: Implement dynamic programming over join subsets. dp[S] = best (plan, cost) for joining the set S of tables. Base: dp[{t}] = access path for t. Transition: dp[S] = min over t in S of cost(dp[S-{t}], access_path(t, join_pred)). Return dp[all_tables].plan.

implementation_phases.name: ANALYZE command implementation
implementation_phases.description: Implement execute_analyze(table_name): full table scan, accumulate min/max/distinct/null counts per column. Write results to sqlite_stat1. Implement ANALYZE for all tables with no argument.

diagrams.title: Cost Model: TableScan vs IndexScan Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side cost computation. TableScan: cost = N (scan all rows). IndexScan: cost = H + sel*N (H=tree height, sel=selectivity, N=table rows). Show crossover point: IndexScan cheaper when H + sel*N < N → sel < (N-H)/N ≈ 1 - H/N. For N=10000, H=14: crossover at sel < 0.9986, effectively almost always for point lookups. Graph: x-axis selectivity 0.0→1.0, y-axis cost, two lines crossing.

diagrams.title: Selectivity Estimation Decision Tree
diagrams.description: Algorithm for estimate_selectivity(expr): if expr is BinOp(Eq, ColRef, Lit) → 1/distinct_count. If BinOp(Lt/Gt, ColRef, Lit) → 0.3 (no histogram). If IS NULL → null_fraction. If AND(a, b) → sel(a) * sel(b). If OR(a,b) → 1 - (1-sel(a))*(1-sel(b)). If no stats available → 0.5. Show decision tree as flowchart.

diagrams.title: Dynamic Programming Join Ordering
diagrams.description: DP table for 3 tables A(100), B(1000), C(500). Base cases: dp[{A}]=100, dp[{B}]=1000, dp[{C}]=500. Two-table: dp[{A,B}]: try A outer (cost=100+100*1000=100100) vs B outer (cost=1000+1000*100=101000) → pick A outer, cost=100100. dp[{A,C}]: try A outer → 100+100*500=50100 vs C outer → 500+500*100=50500 → A outer, cost=50100. Three-table: dp[{A,B,C}] tries all subsets → select minimum total cost plan. Show complete DP table.

modules.name: Transactions and Rollback Journal
modules.description: Implement ACID transactions using SQLite's rollback journal protocol. Before modifying any page, write the original page bytes to the journal file. On COMMIT, flush data pages and delete the journal. On ROLLBACK or crash recovery, replay journal entries to restore original pages.

specs.title: Journal file created on first page write
specs.description: Begin a transaction. Assert journal file does not exist. Modify page 5. Assert journal file exists and contains the journal header magic bytes [0xD9,0xD5,0x05,0xF9,0x20,0xA1,0x63,0xD7].
expected_outputs: journal file exists, first 8 bytes = magic
edge_cases: read-only transaction never creates journal
journal created in same directory as database file

specs.title: Journal entry contains original page bytes before modification
specs.description: Page 7 contains known bytes [0x01..0xFF]. Begin transaction. Write new bytes to page 7. Read journal file. Assert journal entry for page 7 contains the ORIGINAL bytes [0x01..0xFF], not the new bytes.
expected_outputs: journal entry payload = original page 7 bytes
edge_cases: same page written twice in one transaction: only first original written to journal
page never read before write: must read from disk before journaling

specs.title: COMMIT deletes journal file and flushes dirty pages
specs.description: Begin transaction. Modify pages 3 and 7. COMMIT. Assert dirty pages flushed to disk (verify via mock disk write_count ≥ 2). Assert journal file no longer exists. Assert database file contains new page contents.
expected_outputs: disk.write_count ≥ 2, journal_file_exists = false, db pages updated
edge_cases: COMMIT with no modifications: journal not created, no disk writes
flush must occur BEFORE journal deletion (ordering invariant)

specs.title: ROLLBACK restores original pages from journal
specs.description: Insert row into table (modifies B-tree pages). ROLLBACK. Assert database B-tree pages are identical to pre-transaction state. Assert inserted row not visible in subsequent SELECT.
expected_outputs: B-tree pages restored, row not visible
edge_cases: ROLLBACK of empty transaction is no-op
ROLLBACK after partial multi-statement transaction

specs.title: Crash recovery: stale journal replayed on database open
specs.description: Simulate crash: begin transaction, write journal, modify pages in memory but do NOT commit or delete journal. Re-open database. Assert crash recovery detects journal, replays all journal entries, and database is in pre-transaction state.
inputs: stale journal file present on open
expected_outputs: recovery replays journal, database restored to pre-crash state, journal deleted
edge_cases: truncated journal (incomplete entry): treat as if transaction never started — replay only complete entries
journal with page_count=0 (committed): delete without replay

implementation_phases.name: Journal file format: header and entries
implementation_phases.description: Implement JournalHeader { magic: [u8;8], page_count: i32 (-1=hot), page_size: u32, checksum_nonce: u32 }. Implement JournalEntry { page_number: u32, original_data: [u8;PAGE_SIZE], checksum: u32 }. Implement write_header() and append_entry().

implementation_phases.name: Transaction begin/rollback/commit lifecycle
implementation_phases.description: Implement Transaction { journal_path, journaled_pages: HashSet<PageId> }. Implement begin(): create journal file, write hot header (page_count=-1). Implement journal_page(page_id): if not in journaled_pages, read original from disk, append journal entry, add to set.

implementation_phases.name: Integration with buffer pool write path
implementation_phases.description: In BufferPool.mark_dirty(frame_id): call active_transaction.journal_page(page_id) before allowing the dirty flag. This guarantees: journal entry exists before any in-memory modification is visible.

implementation_phases.name: COMMIT: flush ordering and journal deletion
implementation_phases.description: Implement commit(): (1) flush all dirty pages to disk, (2) fsync database file, (3) delete journal file, (4) fsync directory entry. This ordering ensures crash safety: journal exists until data is definitely on disk.

implementation_phases.name: Crash recovery on database open
implementation_phases.description: Implement check_for_journal() on Database::open(). If journal file exists and has page_count=-1 (hot): read journal header, read all complete entries (validate checksum), write original pages back to database file, delete journal. If page_count=0: delete journal (committed). If truncated: replay only complete entries.

diagrams.title: Rollback Journal File Binary Layout
diagrams.description: Exact byte layout of journal file. Header (28 bytes): [0..7] magic (8 bytes), [8..11] page_count i32 BE (-1=hot, 0=committed, N=N pages follow), [12..15] nonce u32 BE, [16..19] initial_pages u32 BE, [20..23] sector_size u32 BE, [24..27] page_size u32 BE. Then N journal entries, each: [0..3] page_number u32 BE, [4..4+PAGE_SIZE-1] original_page_data, [4+PAGE_SIZE..4+PAGE_SIZE+3] checksum u32 BE. Annotate entry size = 4 + PAGE_SIZE + 4.

diagrams.title: Transaction State Machine
diagrams.description: States: IDLE, OPEN_READ (SELECT only, no journal), OPEN_WRITE (journal created, pages being modified), COMMITTING (flushing to disk), ROLLING_BACK (replaying journal). Transitions: BEGIN → OPEN_READ, first write → OPEN_WRITE (create journal), COMMIT → COMMITTING → IDLE (delete journal), ROLLBACK → ROLLING_BACK → IDLE (replay + delete), crash in OPEN_WRITE → on next open → ROLLING_BACK.

diagrams.title: Write Ordering Invariant: Journal Before Data
diagrams.description: Sequence showing the mandatory ordering. VM calls write_page(7, new_data): (1) BufferPool checks journaled_pages[7] — not present. (2) BufferPool reads page 7 original bytes from disk. (3) append_journal_entry(7, original_bytes). (4) fsync journal. (5) Copy new_data into frame. (6) Mark frame dirty. COMMIT sequence: (A) flush all dirty frames to disk. (B) fsync database file. (C) delete journal file. (D) fsync directory. Annotate: if crash between (5) and (A): journal replay restores page 7.

diagrams.title: Crash Recovery Step-by-Step
diagrams.description: Recovery algorithm on Database::open(): Step 1: check if <dbname>-journal exists. Step 2: if not → no recovery needed, proceed. Step 3: read journal header, validate magic bytes. Step 4: if page_count = 0 → committed journal, delete and proceed. Step 5: if page_count = -1 → hot journal. Step 6: read entries sequentially, validate each entry checksum (nonce XOR page_number XOR sum of data words). Step 7: for each valid entry: write original_data to page_number in database file. Step 8: fsync database file. Step 9: delete journal file. Step 10: open database normally. Handle truncated journal: stop at first invalid/incomplete entry.

modules.name: WAL Mode
modules.description: Implement Write-Ahead Logging as an alternative to the rollback journal. In WAL mode, writers append new page versions to the WAL file instead of modifying the database file in place. Readers use the WAL index to find the latest version of each page. Implement the checkpointing process that copies WAL frames back to the database file.

specs.title: WAL frame has correct binary header with checksum
specs.description: Write frame for page 5 with salt=(0x12345678, 0xABCDEF01). Assert frame header: [0..3] page_number=5 u32 BE, [4..7] db_size_after_commit (0 if not last frame), [8..11] salt1=0x12345678, [12..15] salt2=0xABCDEF01, [16..19] checksum1 (computed), [20..23] checksum2 (computed). Assert checksum values match the WAL checksum algorithm applied to (page_number, db_size, salt1, salt2, page_data).
expected_outputs: frame header bytes matching spec, checksum validates
edge_cases: last frame in transaction has db_size > 0
non-last frames have db_size = 0

specs.title: WAL reader finds most recent frame for a page
specs.description: WAL contains frames: frame1(page=3,txn=1), frame2(page=5,txn=1), frame3(page=3,txn=2). Reader at snapshot including txn=2 requests page 3. Assert reader returns frame3 data (most recent version). Reader at snapshot including only txn=1 requests page 3. Assert reader returns frame1 data.
expected_outputs: snapshot2: page 3 = frame3.data; snapshot1: page 3 = frame1.data
edge_cases: page not in WAL at any snapshot → read from database file
concurrent readers each see consistent snapshots

specs.title: Writer appends new frame, does not modify database file
specs.description: Modify page 7 in a WAL-mode transaction. Assert database file page 7 is UNCHANGED. Assert WAL file has grown by (frame_header_size + PAGE_SIZE) bytes. Assert new frame's page_number = 7 and data matches the modification.
expected_outputs: db file unchanged, WAL grew by 4120 bytes, new frame.page=7
edge_cases: multiple modifications to same page in one transaction: only last frame committed

specs.title: Checkpoint copies WAL frames to database file
specs.description: Perform 3 transactions in WAL mode modifying pages 1,2,3,4,5. Assert WAL contains 5+ frames. Execute PRAGMA wal_checkpoint. Assert all frames copied to database file. Assert database file pages 1-5 contain correct data. Assert WAL can be reset to empty (wal_size_after_checkpoint = 0 or header reset).
expected_outputs: db file pages 1-5 = latest WAL frame data, WAL reset
edge_cases: checkpoint with active readers: only checkpoint up to reader's oldest snapshot
checkpoint failure (crash during): WAL still valid for recovery

specs.title: WAL header magic and version on fresh WAL
specs.description: Create a fresh WAL file. Assert first 4 bytes = magic [0x37,0x7F,0x06,0x82] (big-endian) or [0x37,0x7F,0x06,0x83] (little-endian). Assert bytes [4..7] = WAL format version = 3007000. Assert bytes [8..11] = page_size. Assert bytes [12..15] = checkpoint_sequence.
expected_outputs: WAL header bytes matching SQLite WAL spec
edge_cases: WAL with wrong magic rejected and treated as corrupt
page_size mismatch between WAL header and database header → error

implementation_phases.name: WAL file format: header and frame structs
implementation_phases.description: Define WalHeader { magic: u32, file_format: u32, page_size: u32, checkpoint_seq: u32, salt1: u32, salt2: u32, checksum1: u32, checksum2: u32 }. Define WalFrame { page_number: u32, db_size: u32, salt1: u32, salt2: u32, checksum1: u32, checksum2: u32, data: [u8;PAGE_SIZE] }. Implement WAL checksum algorithm.

implementation_phases.name: WAL index (in shared memory)
implementation_phases.description: Implement WalIndex: a hash map from page_number to (frame_offset, transaction_id). In SQLite this is a shared-memory file; for simplicity implement as an in-process HashMap<PageId, (FrameOffset, TxnId)>. Implement lookup(page_id, max_txn) -> Option<FrameOffset>.

implementation_phases.name: WAL writer: append frame on page modification
implementation_phases.description: Implement WalWriter.write_frame(page_id, data, is_commit). Compute checksums using running checksum state. Append frame to WAL file. Update WAL index. On is_commit=true: set db_size field and increment transaction counter.

implementation_phases.name: WAL reader: page lookup with snapshot isolation
implementation_phases.description: Implement WalReader { max_frame: FrameOffset }. In fetch_page: first consult WAL index for latest frame ≤ max_frame. If found: read frame data from WAL file. If not found: read from database file.

implementation_phases.name: Checkpointing
implementation_phases.description: Implement checkpoint(): iterate WAL frames from oldest to newest. For each frame with page_number P: write frame data to database file at page P's offset. After all frames written: fsync database file. Reset WAL index. Write new WAL header with incremented checkpoint_seq.

diagrams.title: WAL Frame Binary Layout
diagrams.description: Exact byte layout of WAL frame (24-byte header + PAGE_SIZE data = 4120 bytes total). [0..3] page_number u32 BE, [4..7] db_size_after_commit u32 BE (0 if not commit frame), [8..11] salt1 u32 BE (from WAL header), [12..15] salt2 u32 BE, [16..19] checksum1 u32 BE (running checksum of all previous frames + this header), [20..23] checksum2 u32 BE, [24..4119] page_data (PAGE_SIZE bytes). Annotate WAL file layout: WAL header (32 bytes) | frame0 | frame1 | ... | frameN. Show WAL header: [0..3] magic, [4..7] format_version, [8..11] page_size, [12..15] checkpoint_seq, [16..19] salt1, [20..23] salt2, [24..27] cksum1, [28..31] cksum2.

diagrams.title: Concurrent Readers and Writer Snapshot Isolation
diagrams.description: Timeline: Reader R1 starts (snapshot at WAL frame 5). Writer W1 appends frames 6,7,8 (transaction). Reader R2 starts (snapshot at frame 8). R1 reads page 3: WAL index lookup → latest frame ≤ 5 for page 3 → frame 2 → return frame 2 data. R2 reads page 3: lookup → latest frame ≤ 8 → frame 7 → return frame 7 data. W1's frames invisible to R1 but visible to R2. Checkpoint: must wait until R1 releases its snapshot (frame 5) before reclaiming WAL space up to frame 5.

diagrams.title: Checkpoint Algorithm Step-by-Step
diagrams.description: PRAGMA wal_checkpoint execution: (1) Determine mxFrame = last committed WAL frame. (2) Determine oldest active reader snapshot = minReadFrame. (3) For frame i = 1 to min(mxFrame, minReadFrame): read frame i from WAL. (4) Write frame_i.page_data to database_file at offset (frame_i.page_number - 1) * PAGE_SIZE. (5) After all eligible frames written: fsync database file. (6) If all frames checkpointed (mxFrame == minReadFrame): reset WAL — overwrite WAL header with incremented checkpoint_seq, reset mxFrame to 0. (7) Next writer starts writing from frame 1 (WAL reuse). Show partial checkpoint case where active readers block full reset.

diagrams.title: WAL Mode vs Rollback Journal: Write Path Comparison
diagrams.description: Two-column comparison. Rollback Journal: before modifying page P → copy P to journal file → modify P in database file → COMMIT: delete journal. WAL Mode: to modify page P → append new version of P to WAL file → database file NOT modified → COMMIT: mark last frame as commit frame → CHECKPOINT (later): copy WAL frames to database file. Key properties table: Rollback=exclusive write lock on DB file; WAL=readers don't block writers. Rollback=journal deleted on commit; WAL=WAL grows until checkpoint. Rollback=crash recovery reads journal; WAL=crash recovery reads WAL.

modules.name: Aggregate Functions and JOIN
modules.description: Implement aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, MIN, MAX, AVG) with GROUP BY using a hash-based accumulator. Implement HAVING for post-aggregation filtering. Implement two-table JOIN using a nested-loop strategy driven by the query planner's join order. Connect all components end-to-end: tokenizer → parser → compiler → VM → B-tree → buffer pool.

specs.title: COUNT(*) on empty table returns 0
specs.description: Execute 'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM t' on an empty table. Assert single row returned with value 0. Assert no B-tree cursor iterations occurred (early termination with count=0).
edge_cases: COUNT(col) with NULLs: NULLs not counted
COUNT(DISTINCT col): counts unique non-NULL values

specs.title: SUM/AVG aggregate over numeric column
specs.description: Table with values: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. SELECT SUM(val), AVG(val), MIN(val), MAX(val). Assert results: SUM=15, AVG=3.0, MIN=1, MAX=5.
edge_cases: SUM of all NULLs returns NULL (not 0)
AVG with integer column returns REAL result
MIN/MAX with mixed types: comparison by type affinity rules

specs.title: GROUP BY produces one row per group with correct aggregates
specs.description: Table: [(alice,10),(bob,20),(alice,30),(bob,40),(carol,50)]. SELECT name, SUM(val) FROM t GROUP BY name. Assert 3 result rows (order independent): (alice,40), (bob,60), (carol,50).
edge_cases: GROUP BY with NULL key: all NULL rows form one group
GROUP BY multiple columns: group key is tuple

specs.title: HAVING filters groups after aggregation
specs.description: Same table as above. SELECT name, SUM(val) FROM t GROUP BY name HAVING SUM(val) > 45. Assert only (bob,60) returned. Alice (40) and carol (50 ≤ 45 fails) filtered.
edge_cases: HAVING without GROUP BY: treats entire table as one group
HAVING can reference aggregate aliases defined in SELECT

specs.title: Two-table nested-loop JOIN produces correct cross product with predicate
specs.description: orders(id,customer_id,amount): [(1,1,100),(2,2,200),(3,1,300)]. customers(id,name): [(1,'alice'),(2,'bob')]. SELECT c.name, o.amount FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id=c.id. Assert 3 result rows: (alice,100),(bob,200),(alice,300).
inputs: orders(3 rows) JOIN customers(2 rows) ON customer_id=c.id
edge_cases: LEFT JOIN: unmatched outer rows produce NULLs for inner columns
JOIN with no matching rows: empty result

specs.title: End-to-end: complex query through full pipeline
specs.description: Execute: 'SELECT c.name, SUM(o.amount) FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id=c.id GROUP BY c.name HAVING SUM(o.amount) > 150 ORDER BY SUM(o.amount) DESC'. Assert result: [(alice,400),(bob,200)].
edge_cases: ORDER BY on aggregate expression
LIMIT applied after HAVING
verify each pipeline stage: tokenize→parse→compile→execute→aggregate→sort

implementation_phases.name: Accumulator structs for each aggregate function
implementation_phases.description: Define AggAccumulator enum: Count { count: i64 }, Sum { sum: Value, saw_non_null: bool }, Avg { sum: f64, count: i64 }, Min { min: Value }, Max { max: Value }. Implement step(value: Value) and finalize() -> Value for each.

implementation_phases.name: Hash-based GROUP BY accumulator map
implementation_phases.description: Implement GroupByMap: HashMap<GroupKey, Vec<AggAccumulator>>. GroupKey = Vec<Value> (the GROUP BY column values). On each row: encode group key, look up or insert accumulator row, call step() on each accumulator. After full scan: iterate map, call finalize(), apply HAVING filter, collect result rows.

implementation_phases.name: VDBE opcodes for aggregates
implementation_phases.description: Add opcodes: OP_AggStep(agg_idx, reg, func_type), OP_AggFinal(agg_idx, reg), OP_GroupBy(key_regs, n). Compiler emits these for aggregate SELECT. VM maintains agg_state: Vec<AggAccumulator>.

implementation_phases.name: Nested-loop JOIN in VDBE
implementation_phases.description: Implement nested-loop join as double cursor loop: outer cursor OP_OpenRead(t1), inner cursor OP_OpenRead(t2). Outer loop: OP_Column(t1, ...), inner loop rewind+scan: OP_Rewind(t2), inner loop: OP_Column(t2, ...), evaluate join predicate, if match OP_ResultRow. OP_Next(t2), OP_Next(t1).

implementation_phases.name: ORDER BY and LIMIT via sort buffer
implementation_phases.description: Implement sort buffer: collect all result rows into Vec<Row>. Sort using ORDER BY key expressions. Apply LIMIT/OFFSET. Emit sorted rows. For large results, implement external sort (spill to temp B-tree) as stretch goal.

diagrams.title: Aggregate Accumulator Architecture
diagrams.description: Class diagram. AggAccumulator trait: step(value), finalize() -> Value. Concrete impls: CountAcc { count: i64 }, SumAcc { sum: Value, saw_non_null: bool }, AvgAcc { sum: f64, count: i64 }, MinAcc { min: Value }, MaxAcc { max: Value }. GroupByMap: HashMap<Vec<Value>, Vec<Box<dyn AggAccumulator>>>. Show how each input row's group key routes to the correct accumulator slot. Finalize phase: iterate map, finalize each accumulator, apply HAVING predicate.

diagrams.title: GROUP BY Hash Pipeline
diagrams.description: Data flow for SELECT name, SUM(val) GROUP BY name. (1) Full table scan → row stream. (2) For each row: extract GROUP BY keys (name) → encode to Vec<Value>. (3) Hash lookup in GroupByMap → get accumulator slot. (4) Call SumAcc.step(val). (5) After scan complete: iterate GroupByMap entries. (6) Call SumAcc.finalize() → Value. (7) Apply HAVING filter. (8) Construct result row (name, sum). (9) Optional sort → emit rows. Annotate memory usage: O(distinct_groups) accumulator slots.

diagrams.title: Nested-Loop JOIN Execution Sequence
diagrams.description: Sequence for orders JOIN customers ON customer_id=c.id. VM: OP_OpenRead(orders, cursor=0) → OP_OpenRead(customers, cursor=1). Outer loop: OP_Rewind(cursor=0) → OP_Column(0, customer_id, r1). Inner loop: OP_Rewind(cursor=1) → OP_Column(1, id, r2) → OP_Ne(r1, r2, skip) → [match] OP_Column(0, amount, r3), OP_Column(1, name, r4) → OP_ResultRow(r3,r4) → OP_Next(cursor=1) → inner loop. OP_Next(cursor=0) → outer loop. Show that inner cursor is rewound for each outer row: O(N*M) complexity.

tdd_accumulated_md:
# TDD

A test-first construction of a relational database engine from raw bytes to query optimizer. Each module isolates one architectural layer so tests encode invariants before implementation: FSM correctness, page-layout binary contracts, crash-safety journal ordering, and WAL frame checksums. Domain profile: data-storage / expert — every on-disk structure gets a byte-level layout diagram; every stateful protocol gets a state-machine diagram.



<!-- TDD_MOD_ID: build-sqlite-m1 -->
# Full Technical Design Specification — SQL Tokenizer
**Module ID:** `build-sqlite-m1`
**Project:** `build-sqlite` — Build Your Own SQLite
**Language:** C (ISO C11)
**Level:** Expert
**Domain:** Data Storage Systems
**Date:** 2026-03-16
---
## 1. Module Charter
The SQL Tokenizer is the entry point of the entire SQLite query pipeline. Every subsequent component — the parser, query planner, and executor — receives its input as a flat array of `Token` values produced by this module. No downstream component ever examines raw SQL bytes again after the tokenizer completes.
The tokenizer operates as a **zero-copy, single-pass finite-state machine** over a caller-owned string buffer. It emits tokens whose `start` pointer addresses bytes inside the original buffer rather than allocating new memory. This design means the tokenizer itself performs **zero heap allocations** under all normal operating conditions; the caller decides how to store the resulting token stream.
**Deliverables this module must produce:**
| Artifact | Path | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Token type definitions | `src/lexer.h` | Public API, enums, structs |
| Tokenizer implementation | `src/lexer.c` | FSM core, keyword table |
| Unit test suite | `tests/test_lexer.c` | 5 spec groups, 35+ assertions |
| Build integration | `Makefile` | `make test-lexer` target |
**Acceptance gate:** All 5 test specifications pass with `make test-lexer` returning exit code 0, and `valgrind --tool=massif` shows zero heap allocations attributed to any `lexer_*` function.
---
## 2. Prerequisite Knowledge
Before implementing this module, the engineer must be comfortable with:
- **Pointer arithmetic in C:** `start + length` must address one byte past the final character of a lexeme without triggering undefined behavior. The buffer must be NUL-terminated or its length known.
- **`const char *` aliasing rules:** The tokenizer never writes through `Token.start`. Any caller that passes a string literal satisfies the contract.
- **Two's-complement integer representation:** Line and column counters are `int`, not `size_t`. At 2³¹ − 1 characters, overflow is theoretically possible but acceptable for SQL input sizes.
- **Binary search (`bsearch`):** The keyword table uses `bsearch` from `<stdlib.h>`. You must understand the comparator contract: negative/zero/positive return values and `const void *` casting.
- **`<ctype.h>` locale sensitivity:** Use only `isalpha`, `isdigit`, `isspace`, `toupper` — all of which are safe on `unsigned char` input when SQL is ASCII. Pass `(unsigned char)` casts consistently to avoid undefined behavior on signed `char` platforms.
---
## 3. File Structure and Creation Order
Create files in the following order. The order matters because later files `#include` earlier ones.
```
build-sqlite/
├── src/
│   ├── lexer.h          ← Step 1: Token types, structs, public API declarations
│   └── lexer.c          ← Step 2: Full implementation
├── tests/
│   └── test_lexer.c     ← Step 3: Test suite
└── Makefile             ← Step 4: Build rules (or amend existing)
```
**Step 1 — `src/lexer.h`** defines all public-facing types and function declarations. It uses an include guard (`#ifndef LEXER_H`). No implementation code lives here.
**Step 2 — `src/lexer.c`** includes `lexer.h` as its first non-comment line. All helper functions (`peek`, `peek_next`, `advance`, `skip_whitespace_and_comments`, `read_string`, `read_number`, `read_identifier_or_keyword`) are declared `static` so they have internal linkage and cannot pollute other translation units.
**Step 3 — `tests/test_lexer.c`** includes only `lexer.h` and `<assert.h>` / `<stdio.h>`. It never includes `lexer.c`. The test binary is compiled by linking `lexer.o` against `test_lexer.o`.
---
## 4. Complete Data Model
### 4.1 Token Type Enumeration
```c
/* src/lexer.h */
#ifndef LEXER_H
#define LEXER_H
typedef enum {
    /* Literals */
    TOK_INTEGER,        /* [0-9]+                        */
    TOK_FLOAT,          /* [0-9]+ '.' [0-9]*             */
    TOK_STRING,         /* '...' with '' escape           */
    TOK_BLOB,           /* X'...' hex blob literal        */
    /* Names */
    TOK_IDENTIFIER,     /* [a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*        */
    TOK_KEYWORD,        /* reserved word (case-insensitive) */
    TOK_QUOTED_ID,      /* "..." double-quoted identifier */
    /* Comparison operators */
    TOK_EQ,             /* =                              */
    TOK_NE,             /* != or <>                       */
    TOK_LT,             /* <                              */
    TOK_GT,             /* >                              */
    TOK_LE,             /* <=                             */
    TOK_GE,             /* >=                             */
    /* Arithmetic operators */
    TOK_PLUS,           /* +                              */
    TOK_MINUS,          /* -  (unary negation at parser)  */
    TOK_STAR,           /* *                              */
    TOK_SLASH,          /* /                              */
    TOK_PERCENT,        /* %                              */
    /* Bitwise / concat */
    TOK_AMPERSAND,      /* &                              */
    TOK_PIPE,           /* |                              */
    TOK_PIPE2,          /* ||  (string concat)            */
    TOK_TILDE,          /* ~                              */
    TOK_LSHIFT,         /* <<                             */
    TOK_RSHIFT,         /* >>                             */
    /* Punctuation */
    TOK_LPAREN,         /* (                              */
    TOK_RPAREN,         /* )                              */
    TOK_COMMA,          /* ,                              */
    TOK_SEMICOLON,      /* ;                              */
    TOK_DOT,            /* .                              */
    /* Parameter binding */
    TOK_QUESTION,       /* ?                              */
    TOK_COLON,          /* :name                          */
    TOK_AT,             /* @name                          */
    TOK_DOLLAR,         /* $name                          */
    /* Sentinels */
    TOK_EOF,            /* end of input                   */
    TOK_ERROR           /* unrecognized character         */
} TokenType;
```
**Design rationale — single `TOK_KEYWORD` vs per-keyword variants:** SQLite 3's real tokenizer uses per-keyword IDs (TK_SELECT, TK_FROM, etc.) to eliminate a second string comparison in the parser. For this learning implementation we use a single `TOK_KEYWORD` and rely on `lexeme_equals(token, "SELECT")` helpers in the parser. This keeps the enum at a comprehensible size without sacrificing correctness; the keyword table lookup already happens once in the tokenizer.
### 4.2 Token Struct — Byte Layout
```c
typedef struct {
    TokenType    type;    /* offset 0, size 4 (enum = int) */
    const char  *start;  /* offset 8, size 8 (64-bit ptr)  */
    int          length; /* offset 16, size 4              */
    int          line;   /* offset 20, size 4              */
    int          col;    /* offset 24, size 4              */
    /* 4 bytes padding to align next struct to 8 */
} Token;
/* sizeof(Token) == 32 on LP64 platforms */
```
> **Expert note — padding:** On a 64-bit LP64 system, `TokenType` is 4 bytes, then 4 bytes of implicit padding precede the 8-byte pointer. Verify with `static_assert(sizeof(Token) == 32, "Token size changed");`. If you ever target a 32-bit platform where pointers are 4 bytes, `sizeof(Token)` will be 20 with no padding. Your code must never assume `sizeof(Token) == 32`; use the macro only as a build-time regression guard.
**Zero-copy invariant:** `start` always points into the caller's original SQL string. The tokenizer never calls `malloc`, `strdup`, or any allocating function. A token lexeme is recovered at any time as:
```c
/* Print a token's text without NUL-termination: */
printf("%.*s", token.length, token.start);
```
### 4.3 Lexer State Struct
```c
typedef struct {
    const char *source;   /* pointer to full SQL string (not owned) */
    int         length;   /* strlen(source) — cached to avoid O(n) calls */
    int         current;  /* index of next character to consume */
    int         line;     /* current line number, 1-based */
    int         col;      /* current column number, 1-based */
} Lexer;
/* sizeof(Lexer) == 24 on LP64 (no padding needed: ptr+int+int+int+int = 8+4+4+4+4) */
```
> **Expert note:** `current` is `int`, not `size_t`. This is intentional: the maximum SQL statement SQLite handles is bounded well below 2³¹ bytes by the `SQLITE_MAX_LENGTH` compile-time limit (default 1 GB, which does exceed INT_MAX on 32-bit but not on 64-bit where `int` is 32 bits and inputs beyond 2 GB are pathological). For production use, change to `int32_t` from `<stdint.h>` and add an overflow guard.
### 4.4 Keyword Table Entry
```c
/* Internal to lexer.c — static linkage only */
typedef struct {
    const char *word;    /* NUL-terminated, uppercase canonical form */
    int         length;  /* strlen(word) — avoids repeated strlen */
} KeywordEntry;
```
The table is a `static const KeywordEntry kw_table[]` sorted lexicographically by `word` (uppercase). This enables `bsearch` in O(log k) where k = number of reserved words.
---
## 5. Public API — Function Contracts
### 5.1 `lexer_init`
```c
void lexer_init(Lexer *lex, const char *source, int length);
```
**Preconditions:**
- `lex != NULL`
- `source != NULL`
- `length >= 0` and equals the number of bytes to tokenize (NUL byte not included in count)
**Postconditions:**
- `lex->source == source` (pointer copy, not ownership transfer)
- `lex->length == length`
- `lex->current == 0`
- `lex->line == 1`
- `lex->col == 1`
**Side effects:** None. No allocation, no I/O.
**Error behavior:** If `source == NULL` and assertions are enabled (debug build), triggers `assert`. In release builds, undefined behavior — callers must validate.
---
### 5.2 `lexer_next_token`
```c
Token lexer_next_token(Lexer *lex);
```
**Preconditions:**
- `lex` was previously initialized with `lexer_init`
- `lex->current <= lex->length`
**Postconditions:**
- Returns a `Token` with `start` pointing into `lex->source`
- `lex->current`, `lex->line`, `lex->col` are advanced past the returned token
- Once `TOK_EOF` is returned, subsequent calls continue to return `TOK_EOF` (idempotent at end-of-input)
- Once `TOK_ERROR` is returned, `lex->current` is advanced by exactly 1 (error recovery: skip unrecognized character)
**Return value:**
- `type == TOK_EOF` when `lex->current == lex->length` before consuming any character
- `type == TOK_ERROR` when current character does not start any valid token
- All other token types when the corresponding pattern is matched
**Complexity:** O(k) per call where k is the length of the lexeme, O(log K) additional for keyword lookup where K = number of reserved words.
**Thread safety:** None. `Lexer` is not protected. External synchronization required for concurrent access.
---
### 5.3 `lexer_tokenize_all`
```c
int lexer_tokenize_all(Lexer *lex, Token *out, int max_tokens);
```
**Preconditions:**
- `lex` initialized
- `out != NULL`, `out` points to at least `max_tokens` elements
- `max_tokens > 0`
**Postconditions:**
- `out[0..n-1]` contain tokens in source order including the final `TOK_EOF`
- Returns `n`, the number of tokens written including `TOK_EOF`
- If `n` would exceed `max_tokens`, stops and returns `max_tokens` (caller-truncated — check `out[max_tokens-1].type != TOK_EOF` to detect)
**Usage pattern:**
```c
Token tokens[512];
Lexer lex;
lexer_init(&lex, sql, (int)strlen(sql));
int count = lexer_tokenize_all(&lex, tokens, 512);
/* tokens[count-1].type == TOK_EOF unless truncated */
```
---
### 5.4 `token_type_name`
```c
const char *token_type_name(TokenType type);
```
Returns a static string (e.g., `"TOK_INTEGER"`, `"TOK_KEYWORD"`) for debug printing. Never returns `NULL` — unknown values return `"TOK_UNKNOWN"`.
---
### 5.5 `lexeme_equals`
```c
int lexeme_equals(const Token *tok, const char *str);
```
Returns 1 if the token's lexeme (case-insensitive) matches `str`, 0 otherwise. Used by the parser to distinguish `SELECT` from `INSERT` without re-scanning.
**Implementation note:** Uses a loop comparing `toupper((unsigned char)tok->start[i])` against `toupper((unsigned char)str[i])` for `i` in `[0, tok->length)`, then checks `str[tok->length] == '\0'`.
---
## 6. Internal Helper Contracts
All helpers are `static` functions in `lexer.c`. They are documented here because the implementation phases build them incrementally.
| Function | Signature | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `at_end` | `static int at_end(const Lexer *l)` | Returns 1 iff `l->current >= l->length` |
| `peek` | `static char peek(const Lexer *l)` | Returns `l->source[l->current]` without advancing; returns `'\0'` if at end |
| `peek_next` | `static char peek_next(const Lexer *l)` | Returns `l->source[l->current + 1]`; returns `'\0'` if `current + 1 >= length` |
| `advance` | `static char advance(Lexer *l)` | Returns current char, increments `current`; updates `line`/`col` on `'\n'` |
| `match` | `static int match(Lexer *l, char expected)` | Advances and returns 1 if `peek(l) == expected`; else returns 0 |
| `make_token` | `static Token make_token(TokenType t, const char *start, int len, int line, int col)` | Constructs and returns a `Token` |
| `skip_whitespace_and_comments` | `static void skip_whitespace_and_comments(Lexer *l)` | Skips spaces, tabs, CR, LF, `--` comments, `/* */` comments |
| `read_string` | `static Token read_string(Lexer *l, int line, int col)` | Reads single-quoted string with `''` escape; returns `TOK_STRING` or `TOK_ERROR` |
| `read_quoted_id` | `static Token read_quoted_id(Lexer *l, int line, int col)` | Reads double-quoted identifier; returns `TOK_QUOTED_ID` or `TOK_ERROR` |
| `read_number` | `static Token read_number(Lexer *l, int line, int col)` | Reads integer or float; returns `TOK_INTEGER` or `TOK_FLOAT` |
| `read_identifier_or_keyword` | `static Token read_identifier_or_keyword(Lexer *l, int line, int col)` | Reads bare name, classifies as `TOK_IDENTIFIER` or `TOK_KEYWORD` |
| `read_blob` | `static Token read_blob(Lexer *l, int line, int col)` | Reads `X'...'` blob literal; returns `TOK_BLOB` or `TOK_ERROR` |
| `keyword_lookup` | `static int keyword_lookup(const char *text, int length)` | Returns 1 if text (case-insensitive) is a SQL keyword, 0 otherwise |
---
## 7. FSM Specification
{{DIAGRAM:tdd-diag-1}}
*Figure: Lexer finite-state machine. Each node is a named state; edges show the input character class or condition that triggers the transition.*
### 7.1 State Enumeration
The FSM is implemented as a `switch` dispatch in `lexer_next_token`, not as an explicit state variable. The "state" is implicit in which `case` branch is executing. The logical states are:
| State Name | Entry Condition | Exit Transitions |
|---|---|---|
| `S_START` | After `skip_whitespace_and_comments` | All first-character cases |
| `S_STRING` | `peek == '\''` | `''` → stay, `'` → emit `TOK_STRING`, EOF → `TOK_ERROR` |
| `S_QUOTED_ID` | `peek == '"'` | `""` → stay, `"` → emit `TOK_QUOTED_ID`, EOF → `TOK_ERROR` |
| `S_NUMBER` | `isdigit(peek)` | `.` → `S_FLOAT`, non-digit → emit `TOK_INTEGER` |
| `S_FLOAT` | `peek == '.'` while in `S_NUMBER` | non-digit → emit `TOK_FLOAT` |
| `S_IDENT` | `isalpha(peek) \|\| peek == '_'` | non-`[a-zA-Z0-9_]` → keyword check → emit |
| `S_BLOB` | `(peek == 'X' \|\| peek == 'x') && peek_next == '\''` | same as `S_STRING` but prefixed |
| `S_LINE_COMMENT` | `peek == '-' && peek_next == '-'` | `'\n'` or EOF → return to `S_START` |
| `S_BLOCK_COMMENT` | `peek == '/' && peek_next == '*'` | `'*/'` → return to `S_START`, EOF → `TOK_ERROR` |
| `S_OP` | Any operator character | 1 or 2 chars consumed → emit operator token |
### 7.2 Character Class Dispatch Table
The first character of each token determines the dispatch branch:
| First Char | Branch |
|---|---|
| `isspace(c)` | `skip_whitespace_and_comments` then restart |
| `isdigit(c)` | `read_number` |
| `isalpha(c)` or `'_'` | `read_identifier_or_keyword` |
| `'X'` or `'x'` followed by `'\''` | `read_blob` |
| `'\''` | `read_string` |
| `'"'` | `read_quoted_id` |
| `'='` | emit `TOK_EQ` |
| `'!'` + `'='` | emit `TOK_NE` |
| `'!'` (alone) | emit `TOK_ERROR` |
| `'<'` + `'='` | emit `TOK_LE` |
| `'<'` + `'>'` | emit `TOK_NE` |
| `'<'` + `'<'` | emit `TOK_LSHIFT` |
| `'<'` (alone) | emit `TOK_LT` |
| `'>'` + `'='` | emit `TOK_GE` |
| `'>'` + `'>'` | emit `TOK_RSHIFT` |
| `'>'` (alone) | emit `TOK_GT` |
| `'+'` | emit `TOK_PLUS` |
| `'-'` + `'-'` | `skip_whitespace_and_comments` (line comment) |
| `'-'` (alone) | emit `TOK_MINUS` |
| `'*'` | emit `TOK_STAR` |
| `'/'` + `'*'` | `skip_whitespace_and_comments` (block comment) |
| `'/'` (alone) | emit `TOK_SLASH` |
| `'%'` | emit `TOK_PERCENT` |
| `'&'` | emit `TOK_AMPERSAND` |
| `'\|'` + `'\|'` | emit `TOK_PIPE2` |
| `'\|'` (alone) | emit `TOK_PIPE` |
| `'~'` | emit `TOK_TILDE` |
| `'('` | emit `TOK_LPAREN` |
| `')'` | emit `TOK_RPAREN` |
| `','` | emit `TOK_COMMA` |
| `';'` | emit `TOK_SEMICOLON` |
| `'.'` | emit `TOK_DOT` |
| `'?'` | emit `TOK_QUESTION` |
| `':'` | emit `TOK_COLON` |
| `'@'` | emit `TOK_AT` |
| `'$'` | emit `TOK_DOLLAR` |
| `'\0'` / end | emit `TOK_EOF` |
| anything else | advance 1, emit `TOK_ERROR` |
### 7.3 Multi-Character Operator Lookahead
```
advance()          ← consume first char
c = that char
switch(c):
  case '<':
    if match(l, '='):   return TOK_LE   (consumed 2 chars: '<', '=')
    if match(l, '>'):   return TOK_NE   (consumed 2 chars: '<', '>')
    if match(l, '<'):   return TOK_LSHIFT
    return TOK_LT       (consumed 1 char)
  ...
```
`match(l, expected)` peeks at `l->source[l->current]` without advancing unless it equals `expected`. This is the canonical 1-character lookahead pattern from Crafting Interpreters adapted for C.
### 7.4 String Literal FSM Detail
{{DIAGRAM:tdd-diag-2}}
*Figure: String literal sub-FSM handling single-quote escape sequences (`''`) and unterminated string errors.*
```
State: reading a single-quoted string
  start = l->source + l->current - 1   (points at opening quote)
  loop:
    if at_end:
      return TOK_ERROR  (unterminated string)
    c = advance()
    if c == '\'':
      if peek(l) == '\'':    ← SQLite escape: '' inside string
        advance()            ← consume second quote, stay in loop
      else:
        break                ← closing quote, exit loop
  length = (l->source + l->current) - start
  return make_token(TOK_STRING, start, length, saved_line, saved_col)
```
**Invariant:** `token.start` points at the opening `'`; `token.length` includes both surrounding quotes and all internal content. The parser is responsible for stripping delimiters and unescaping `''` → `'` before value use.
### 7.5 Keyword Lookup Detail
{{DIAGRAM:tdd-diag-3}}
*Figure: Keyword recognition flow — uppercasing scratch buffer, binary search, identifier vs. keyword classification.*
```c
static int keyword_lookup(const char *text, int length) {
    /* Stack-allocate scratch buffer; keywords <= 16 chars in SQLite */
    if (length > 16) return 0;
    char upper[17];
    for (int i = 0; i < length; i++)
        upper[i] = (char)toupper((unsigned char)text[i]);
    upper[length] = '\0';
    /* bsearch over sorted KeywordEntry table */
    KeywordEntry key = { upper, length };
    KeywordEntry *found = bsearch(&key, kw_table,
                                  KW_TABLE_SIZE,
                                  sizeof(KeywordEntry),
                                  kw_compare);
    return found != NULL;
}
```
`kw_compare` compares by `strcmp` of `.word` fields. The table must be sorted in the same collation order (strcmp = ASCII order for uppercase strings).

## 8. Error Handling Matrix
| Error Condition | Token Emitted | Recovery Action | Test Verifies |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unterminated string `'abc` | `TOK_ERROR` | Advance to EOF; subsequent call returns `TOK_EOF` | Spec 4a |
| Unterminated block comment `/* abc` | `TOK_ERROR` | Advance to EOF | Spec 4b |
| Bare `!` (not `!=`) | `TOK_ERROR` | Advance 1 char | Spec 4c |
| Invalid hex blob `X'GG'` | `TOK_ERROR` | Advance to closing `'` or EOF | Spec 4d |
| Null byte in input | `TOK_EOF` | Treat as end-of-input | — |
| Empty string input | `TOK_EOF` on first call | — | Spec 1 (edge) |
| Number overflow (value) | Not detected here | Tokenizer emits `TOK_INTEGER` regardless; value check at parse/bind time | — |
**Error token invariant:** A `TOK_ERROR` token has `start` pointing at the first offending character and `length` equal to the number of characters consumed (1 for single unrecognized chars, full length for unterminated literals). This gives the error reporter enough information to display:
```c
fprintf(stderr, "Lexer error at line %d col %d: unexpected '%.*s'\n",
        tok.line, tok.col, tok.length, tok.start);
```

## 9. `advance` and Line Tracking
```c
static char advance(Lexer *l) {
    char c = l->source[l->current++];
    if (c == '\n') {
        l->line++;
        l->col = 1;
    } else {
        l->col++;
    }
    return c;
}
```
**Column semantics:** `col` is 1-based. After `lexer_init`, `col == 1`. The first character consumed by `advance` will be at column 1. After a newline, `col` resets to 1 so the next character is column 1 of the new line.
**Token position capture:** Before calling any `read_*` function or emitting a single-char token, capture `saved_line = l->line; saved_col = l->col;`. These saved values become `token.line` and `token.col`. This ensures the token position reflects where the token **starts**, not where the lexer position is after consuming it.
---
## 10. `skip_whitespace_and_comments` Algorithm
```
loop:
  while peek is whitespace: advance()
  if peek == '-' and peek_next == '-':
    while not at_end and peek != '\n': advance()
    continue loop     ← might be more whitespace after comment
  if peek == '/' and peek_next == '*':
    advance(); advance()    ← consume '/', '*'
    while not at_end:
      if peek == '*' and peek_next == '/':
        advance(); advance()    ← consume '*', '/'
        break inner while
      advance()
    else:              ← loop fell off end → unterminated comment
      store error flag on lexer? or emit TOK_ERROR in next_token
    continue loop
  break outer loop    ← no whitespace, no comment
```
**Unterminated block comment handling:** The simplest correct approach is: after the inner while exits via EOF (not via `*/`), set a `lex->error_pending = 1` flag and the starting position of the comment. Then `lexer_next_token` checks `error_pending` before the main dispatch and returns a `TOK_ERROR` immediately. This avoids tangling error emission into the whitespace skipping function.

**Goal:** `lexer_next_token` correctly handles whitespace, all single-character tokens, and EOF.

## 12. Test Specifications
### Test Spec 1 — FSM State Transitions
**Name:** `test_fsm_transitions`
**Verifies:** Each major FSM branch is exercised in isolation.
```c
void test_fsm_transitions(void) {
    Lexer l;
    Token t;
    /* Single operator tokens */
    lexer_init(&l, "(", 1);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_LPAREN);
    assert(t.length == 1);
    assert(t.line == 1 && t.col == 1);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_EOF);
    /* Two-char operator: >= */
    lexer_init(&l, ">=", 2);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_GE);
    assert(t.length == 2);
    /* Two-char operator: <> */
    lexer_init(&l, "<>", 2);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_NE);
    /* != also maps to TOK_NE */
    lexer_init(&l, "!=", 2);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_NE);
    /* || concat */
    lexer_init(&l, "||", 2);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_PIPE2);
    /* Whitespace skipped, position tracked */
    lexer_init(&l, "  ;", 3);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_SEMICOLON);
    assert(t.col == 3);
    /* Newline increments line counter */
    lexer_init(&l, "\n;", 2);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_SEMICOLON);
    assert(t.line == 2 && t.col == 1);
    /* Empty input */
    lexer_init(&l, "", 0);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_EOF);
    /* Repeated EOF calls are safe */
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_EOF);
    printf("PASS: test_fsm_transitions\n");
}
```

### Test Spec 2 — Keyword Case-Insensitivity
**Name:** `test_keyword_case_insensitive`
**Verifies:** `SELECT`, `select`, `SeLeCt`, `NULL`, `null` all produce `TOK_KEYWORD`; a non-keyword identifier produces `TOK_IDENTIFIER`.
```c
void test_keyword_case_insensitive(void) {
    const char *variants[] = { "SELECT", "select", "SeLeCt", "sElEcT", NULL };
    for (int i = 0; variants[i]; i++) {
        Lexer l;
        lexer_init(&l, variants[i], (int)strlen(variants[i]));
        Token t = lexer_next_token(&l);
        assert(t.type == TOK_KEYWORD);
        assert(lexeme_equals(&t, "SELECT"));
        assert(lexer_next_token(&l).type == TOK_EOF);
    }
    /* NULL is a keyword */
    Lexer l;
    lexer_init(&l, "NULL", 4);
    Token t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_KEYWORD);
    assert(lexeme_equals(&t, "NULL"));
    /* Identifier that starts like a keyword but isn't */
    lexer_init(&l, "selects", 7);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_IDENTIFIER);
    assert(t.length == 7);
    /* Identifier with underscore */
    lexer_init(&l, "my_table", 8);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_IDENTIFIER);
    /* Keyword followed immediately by punctuation */
    lexer_init(&l, "FROM(", 5);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_KEYWORD);
    assert(t.length == 4);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_LPAREN);
    printf("PASS: test_keyword_case_insensitive\n");
}
```

### Test Spec 3 — Full Statement Tokenization
**Name:** `test_full_select_statement`
**Verifies:** A realistic SQL statement tokenizes to the expected sequence.
```c
void test_full_select_statement(void) {
    const char *sql = "SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE age >= 18;";
    Token tokens[64];
    Lexer l;
    lexer_init(&l, sql, (int)strlen(sql));
    int n = lexer_tokenize_all(&l, tokens, 64);
    /* Verify sequence */
    int i = 0;
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_KEYWORD);  assert(lexeme_equals(&tokens[i], "SELECT")); i++;
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_IDENTIFIER); assert(tokens[i].length == 2); i++; /* id */
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_COMMA); i++;
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_IDENTIFIER); assert(tokens[i].length == 4); i++; /* name */
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_KEYWORD);  assert(lexeme_equals(&tokens[i], "FROM")); i++;
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_IDENTIFIER); assert(tokens[i].length == 5); i++; /* users */
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_KEYWORD);  assert(lexeme_equals(&tokens[i], "WHERE")); i++;
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_IDENTIFIER); assert(tokens[i].length == 3); i++; /* age */
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_GE); i++;
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_INTEGER); assert(tokens[i].length == 2); i++; /* 18 */
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_SEMICOLON); i++;
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_EOF); i++;
    assert(i == n);
    /* Zero-copy: all starts point into original sql buffer */
    for (int j = 0; j < n - 1; j++) { /* exclude EOF */
        assert(tokens[j].start >= sql);
        assert(tokens[j].start + tokens[j].length <= sql + strlen(sql));
    }
    /* Test with comment */
    const char *sql2 = "SELECT -- pick all\n* FROM t;";
    lexer_init(&l, sql2, (int)strlen(sql2));
    n = lexer_tokenize_all(&l, tokens, 64);
    assert(tokens[0].type == TOK_KEYWORD); /* SELECT */
    assert(tokens[1].type == TOK_STAR);     /* * (comment skipped) */
    assert(tokens[2].type == TOK_KEYWORD); /* FROM */
    /* String literal */
    const char *sql3 = "WHERE name = 'O''Brien'";
    lexer_init(&l, sql3, (int)strlen(sql3));
    Token t;
    t = lexer_next_token(&l); assert(t.type == TOK_KEYWORD);   /* WHERE */
    t = lexer_next_token(&l); assert(t.type == TOK_IDENTIFIER); /* name */
    t = lexer_next_token(&l); assert(t.type == TOK_EQ);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_STRING);
    assert(t.length == 10); /* 'O''Brien' = 10 chars incl quotes */
    printf("PASS: test_full_select_statement\n");
}
```

```c
    /* After error, next call should return TOK_EOF */
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_EOF);
    /* 4b: Unterminated block comment */
    lexer_init(&l, "/* no end", 9);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_ERROR);
    /* 4c: Bare ! not followed by = */
    lexer_init(&l, "!", 1);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_ERROR);
    assert(t.length == 1);
    /* 4d: Error recovery — tokens after error are still valid */
    lexer_init(&l, "@ SELECT", 8);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    /* '@' alone with no following name — tokenizer emits TOK_AT (not error) */
    assert(t.type == TOK_AT);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_KEYWORD);
    /* 4e: Error position tracking */
    lexer_init(&l, "x = `bad`", 9);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l); assert(t.type == TOK_IDENTIFIER); /* x */
    t = lexer_next_token(&l); assert(t.type == TOK_EQ);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l); assert(t.type == TOK_ERROR);
    assert(t.col == 5); /* backtick at column 5 */
    /* 4f: Float number */
    lexer_init(&l, "3.14", 4);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_FLOAT);
    assert(t.length == 4);
    printf("PASS: test_error_handling\n");
}
```
---
### Test Spec 5 — Zero Heap Allocation
**Name:** `test_zero_heap_allocation`
**Verifies:** The tokenizer never calls `malloc`, `calloc`, `realloc`, or `strdup`.
**Primary method — Valgrind/Massif:**
```bash
# In Makefile:
valgrind-test-lexer: test_lexer
    valgrind --tool=massif --pages-as-heap=yes \
             --massif-out-file=massif.out ./test_lexer
    ms_print massif.out | grep -A5 "SQL Tokenizer"
    # Verify: total heap allocation attributed to lexer_* = 0 bytes
```
**Secondary method — malloc interception (compile-time):**
```c
/* In test_lexer.c only, BEFORE any includes: */
#include <stdlib.h>
static int g_malloc_count = 0;
void *__wrap_malloc(size_t size) {
    g_malloc_count++;
    return __real_malloc(size);
}
void test_zero_heap_allocation(void) {
    g_malloc_count = 0;
    Token tokens[1024];
    Lexer l;
    const char *sql = "SELECT a, b, c FROM t WHERE x = 1 AND y > 2.5;";
    lexer_init(&l, sql, (int)strlen(sql));
    lexer_tokenize_all(&l, tokens, 1024);
    assert(g_malloc_count == 0);
    printf("PASS: test_zero_heap_allocation (malloc_count=%d)\n", g_malloc_count);
}
```
**Compile with:** `gcc -Wl,--wrap=malloc -o test_lexer test_lexer.c lexer.o`
**Invariant that guarantees the test passes:** `Lexer` is stack-allocated by the caller. `lexer_init` performs only pointer copies and integer assignments. All `read_*` functions compute `start = &lex->source[saved_index]` and `length = lex->current - saved_index`. No function in `lexer.c` contains the text `malloc`, `calloc`, `realloc`, `strdup`, or `strndup`. Enforce with:
```bash
grep -n "malloc\|calloc\|realloc\|strdup" src/lexer.c && echo "FAIL: heap alloc found" && exit 1 || echo "OK: no heap alloc"
```
Add this grep to the `make test-lexer` recipe as a pre-flight check.

## 14. Performance Targets
The tokenizer runs in the hot path of every query execution. Target performance on a modern 64-bit CPU (3 GHz):
| Metric | Target | Measurement Method |
|---|---|---|
| Single token, simple operator | ≤ 5 ns | `clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC)` over 10M iterations |
| Single token, identifier + keyword lookup | ≤ 20 ns | Same — includes binary search |
| Single token, string literal (10 chars) | ≤ 15 ns | Same |
| Full `SELECT ... WHERE ...` (12 tokens) | ≤ 150 ns | End-to-end via `lexer_tokenize_all` |
| 1 MB SQL file (batch) | ≤ 400 MB/s throughput | `wc -c` / elapsed |
**Performance microbenchmark template:**
```c
#include <time.h>
#define BENCH_ITERS 10000000
void bench_next_token(void) {
    const char *sql = "SELECT id FROM users WHERE age >= 18;";
    int len = (int)strlen(sql);
    Token tokens[64];
    struct timespec t0, t1;
    clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &t0);
    for (int i = 0; i < BENCH_ITERS; i++) {
        Lexer l;
        lexer_init(&l, sql, len);
        lexer_tokenize_all(&l, tokens, 64);
    }
    clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &t1);
    long ns = (t1.tv_sec - t0.tv_sec) * 1000000000L
            + (t1.tv_nsec - t0.tv_nsec);
    printf("%.1f ns/token-sequence over %d iters\n",
           (double)ns / BENCH_ITERS, BENCH_ITERS);
}
```
**Optimization notes (do not premature-optimize; apply only if bench fails):**
- Replace `bsearch` with a perfect hash (gperf) for keyword lookup — eliminates O(log k) and avoids `toupper` loop
- Ensure the hot `advance` function inlines: mark with `static inline`
- Use `__builtin_expect(at_end(l), 0)` around the EOF guard inside tight inner loops
- Profile with `perf stat` to confirm instruction cache vs. branch mispredictions are the bottleneck before rewriting
---
## 15. Dependency and Integration Notes
This module (`build-sqlite-m1`) has **no upstream dependencies** within the project. It is the first module to implement.
Downstream modules that consume `Token` arrays:
| Module | Dependency |
|---|---|
| `build-sqlite-m2` SQL Parser | Calls `lexer_tokenize_all`; reads `token.type`, `token.start`, `token.length` |
| `build-sqlite-m3` AST Builder | Reads `token.line`, `token.col` for error messages |
| All subsequent modules | Indirectly via the parse tree |
**Stability contract:** Once M2 development begins, the `Token` struct layout and `TokenType` enum must not be changed without a version bump. Adding new token types (e.g., `TOK_BLOB` if deferred) is backward-compatible; removing or reordering enum values is not.
---
## 16. Common Mistakes and Mitigations
| Mistake | Symptom | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Forgetting `(unsigned char)` cast in `isdigit`/`toupper` | UB on platforms where `char` is signed and input contains non-ASCII bytes | Always cast: `isdigit((unsigned char)c)` |
| Off-by-one in `length` calculation | Token appears to include trailing character | `length = current - start_index`, not `current - start_index + 1` |
| `keyword_lookup` called with `length > 16` | Stack buffer overflow in `upper[17]` | Explicit `if (length > 16) return 0;` guard at function entry |
| `kw_table` not sorted | `bsearch` returns spurious misses | Add sort-verification test (Spec 2 implicitly catches many; explicit check catches all) |
| `peek` called after `at_end` | Reads `source[length]` — valid only if NUL-terminated | `peek` returns `'\0'` when `at_end` — this is safe with NUL-terminated strings; add guard |
| Saving `start` after first `advance` call | Token start offset by 1 | Save start index BEFORE calling `advance()` |
| Not resetting `col` on `'\n'` in `advance` | Column numbers wrong after newlines | The `if (c == '\n') { line++; col = 1; }` branch must set `col = 1`, not `col = 0` |

# Module M2 — Recursive-Descent Parser and AST
**Project:** Build Your Own SQLite | **Module ID:** `build-sqlite-m2`
**Language:** C (ISO C11) | **Depends On:** `build-sqlite-m1` (SQL Tokenizer)
**Difficulty:** Expert | **Estimated Effort:** 10–14 hours
---
## 0. Context and Purpose
M1 delivered a complete tokenizer: given a SQL string, `lexer_tokenize_all` returns a flat `Token[]` with every lexeme classified. M2 picks up that token stream and assigns grammatical structure. The output is an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** — a tree of typed C structs where each node represents one syntactic construct (a statement, an expression, a column definition) rather than one character range.
After this module:
- A `SELECT a, b FROM t WHERE x > 1 ORDER BY a LIMIT 10` string becomes a tree you can walk, rewrite, validate, or compile to bytecode.
- Every subsequent module (planner, VM, schema manager) accepts `AstNode*` as input, never raw SQL text.
The parser is **hand-written and recursive-descent**. No parser generators. No grammar tables generated by a tool. Every production rule is a C function. Expression parsing uses **Pratt / precedence-climbing** to handle operator precedence without mutual recursion per precedence level.
---
## 1. Upstream API Contract (M1 Summary)
The parser consumes the output of M1. Reproduce the key declarations here so M2 can be built with only this document.
```c
/* src/lexer.h (produced by M1 — do not modify) */
typedef enum {
    TOK_EOF = 0,
    TOK_SEMICOLON, TOK_COMMA, TOK_LPAREN, TOK_RPAREN,
    TOK_DOT, TOK_STAR,
    TOK_EQ, TOK_NEQ, TOK_LT, TOK_LE, TOK_GT, TOK_GE,
    TOK_PLUS, TOK_MINUS, TOK_SLASH, TOK_PERCENT,
    TOK_INTEGER_LIT, TOK_REAL_LIT, TOK_STRING_LIT,
    TOK_IDENTIFIER,
    TOK_KEYWORD,        /* SELECT INSERT CREATE DROP TABLE WHERE … */
    TOK_ERROR
} TokenType;
typedef struct {
    TokenType   type;
    const char *start;  /* pointer into original source; NOT null-terminated */
    int         length;
    int         line;
    int         col;
} Token;
typedef struct { /* opaque — parser does not call lexer functions directly */
    const char *src;
    int         pos, len, line, col;
} Lexer;
/* Batch tokenize — returns heap-allocated array; caller frees */
Token *lexer_tokenize_all(const char *src, int *out_count);
/* Case-insensitive lexeme comparison against a null-terminated keyword */
int lexeme_equals(const Token *tok, const char *kw);
```
**Rule:** After calling `lexer_tokenize_all`, the parser never calls any `lexer_*` function. It works exclusively on the `Token[]` array.
